proposals.title: Quantifying the Generalizability of Polygenic Risk Scores and Clinical Risk Models: A Systematic Search for Stable, Homogeneous Risk Prediction Across Populations
proposals.motivation: A dominant theme in epidemiology and genetic epidemiology is the identification of heterogeneity in risk associations and the poor transportability of risk models across populations. This focus, while crucial, may overshadow the identification of conditions and modeling contexts where risk prediction is remarkably stable. Identifying such 'homogeneous' scenarios is critical for deploying reliable, generalizable clinical tools and for understanding the fundamental biology of diseases where genetic and environmental effects are consistent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for specific disease endpoints, particularly those with strong, conserved biological pathways (e.g., vertebral fracture risk linked to bone mineral density), and when using specific modeling approaches (e.g., leveraging instrumental variables or Mendelian randomization), baseline risk prediction will demonstrate high generalizability and homogeneity across diverse study populations and ancestries.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-epidemiological analysis of existing large-scale consortium data for diseases studied by Ioannidis's group (e.g., fracture, obesity). Using individual-level data from cohorts of diverse ancestries, we will fit established risk models (e.g., PRS from GWAS, clinical risk scores) in a 'training' set and rigorously test generalizability in held-out 'validation' sets. We will employ metrics of calibration, discrimination, and heterogeneity (I²) to quantify stability. The core technical approach will involve two-stage least squares and Mendelian Randomization frameworks to estimate causal effects that are less prone to confounding, testing their homogeneity across populations as per Ioannidis's recent work on genetic contributions to population differences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, empirical catalog of disease-risk model combinations where prediction is homogeneous, offering a counterpoint to the literature on heterogeneity. It will deliver practical guidance on when risk models can be confidently applied across settings. Furthermore, it will refine methods for assessing generalizability, moving beyond simple transportability checks to formal tests of homogeneity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard research question from 'where does prediction fail?' to 'where does it robustly succeed?', applying a rigorous meta-epidemiological framework championed by Ioannidis to the domain of genetic and clinical risk prediction. It directly integrates his recent methodological work on genetic variants and population differences to search for stability rather than divergence.

proposals.title: Quantifying Baseline Risk Heterogeneity Across Clinical Domains: A Systematic Application of Extreme Quartile Odds/Rate Ratio (EQuOR/EQuRR) Metrics
proposals.motivation: The heterogeneity of baseline risk is a critical, often overlooked factor that can dramatically alter the absolute benefit or harm of an intervention, even when relative effects are constant. Pre-2026, methods to systematically quantify this heterogeneity across diverse clinical conditions are lacking. Applying the EQuOR/EQuRR framework beyond its initial derivation contexts would provide a standardized metric to identify conditions where risk stratification is most crucial for personalized medicine and trial interpretation.
proposals.hypothesis: The EQuOR/EQuRR metrics will reveal substantial and variable degrees of baseline risk heterogeneity across different medical conditions, and this heterogeneity will be associated with specific disease etiologies, such as those with strong polygenic risk architectures versus those driven by major environmental factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic analysis of large, pre-existing cohort studies and biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, FinnGen) covering diverse outcomes including cardiovascular events, fractures, severe infections, and psychiatric disorders. For each condition, we will calculate EQuOR/EQuRR by first deriving a multivariable baseline risk score using established pre-2026 risk factors (e.g., age, sex, genetic risk scores, clinical biomarkers). We will then compare the odds or rate of the outcome between the extreme quartiles (top vs. bottom 25%) of this risk distribution. Analyses will be stratified by genetic and non-genetic risk components to dissect contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first cross-domain atlas of baseline risk heterogeneity, providing empirical estimates of where absolute risk gradients are steepest. It will offer a methodological framework for assessing when 'one-size-fits-all' intervention thresholds are inadequate. The findings will directly inform the design of risk-stratified trials and the prioritization of conditions for precision public health efforts.
proposals.novelty: While EQuOR/EQuRR has been proposed, its systematic application across a wide spectrum of diseases to compare heterogeneity patterns is novel. Linking the degree of heterogeneity to specific etiological profiles (e.g., polygenic score explainability) moves beyond description towards mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Predicting Clinical Trial Cohort Membership from Baseline Risk Profiles: A Machine Learning Approach to Characterizing Trial Generalizability
proposals.motivation: The generalizability of clinical trial results is a cornerstone of evidence-based medicine, yet it is often assessed qualitatively. A quantitative, data-driven method to characterize how trial populations differ from each other and from target populations is critically needed. This problem directly addresses concerns about the transportability of treatment effects, a key issue in meta-research and applied epidemiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning models can accurately predict the specific clinical trial from which an individual originated based solely on their predicted baseline risk profile for the primary outcome, and that the features driving this prediction reveal systematic differences in enrollment criteria and participant characteristics across trials.
proposals.method: We will utilize individual participant data from multiple large, published randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and cohort studies for a condition like osteoporotic fracture, where polygenic risk scores (PRS) and clinical risk factors are well-established (pre-2026). For each individual, we will compute a validated baseline risk score (e.g., FRAX score augmented with PRS). Using these risk scores and constituent features as input, we will train supervised classification models (e.g., random forests, gradient boosting, and regularized logistic regression) to predict trial membership. Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validation, and feature importance metrics (e.g., SHAP values) will identify the risk profile components most discriminatory between trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for assessing the heterogeneity and representativeness of trial populations. It will contribute methodological tools for meta-research by inverting the traditional risk prediction paradigm to audit trial design and enrollment. The findings could inform the design of future trials and the interpretation of meta-analyses by explicitly mapping the 'risk space' of different study samples.
proposals.novelty: While risk prediction is standard, using it to classify trial origin is an inverse problem rarely tackled. This approach moves beyond comparing mean characteristics to model the multivariate distribution of risk, offering a more nuanced view of between-trial differences.

proposals.title: Testing the Lifespan Invariance of Genetic Effects on Bone Mineral Density: A Multi-Cohort Age-Stratified Mendelian Randomization and Interaction Analysis
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for bone mineral density (BMD) and fracture risk have identified numerous loci, but these studies typically assume genetic effects are constant across the lifespan. This assumption is untested and biologically implausible, given known age-related changes in bone remodeling. If genetic effects on BMD are age-dependent, it could significantly impact the accuracy of lifetime fracture risk prediction and the understanding of osteoporosis pathophysiology.
proposals.hypothesis: The polygenic influence and the effect sizes of key established BMD-associated genetic variants, particularly those in loci like 16q24, are not static but vary significantly with age.
proposals.method: We will perform a coordinated meta-analysis of individual-level genetic and phenotypic data from large, deeply phenotyped cohorts with wide age ranges (e.g., UK Biobank, Framingham, AGES-Reykjavik). Using age-stratified regression models (e.g., <50, 50-70, >70 years), we will test for genotype-by-age interaction effects on BMD (lumbar spine and femoral neck DXA) for known GWAS hits. We will employ Mendelian Randomization (MR) frameworks, including age-stratified two-sample MR, to assess whether the causal effect of genetically predicted BMD on fracture risk changes with age, while rigorously evaluating potential violations of MR assumptions in different age strata.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, direct test of the lifespan stability of genetic effects on a critical musculoskeletal trait. It will clarify whether polygenic risk scores for BMD require age-specific weighting for optimal clinical prediction. Furthermore, it will identify genetic variants with age-varying effects, offering new insights into the biological pathways active in bone aging versus development.
proposals.novelty: While previous GWAS have adjusted for age as a covariate, this study explicitly models genetic effects as a function of age, moving beyond the standard assumption of effect homogeneity. It integrates interaction analysis with a causal inference (MR) framework to test for age-varying *causal* effects, a methodologically rigorous approach not yet applied to BMD genetics.

proposals.title: A Multi-Layered Interaction Analysis of Genetic Variants and Environmental Exposures on Bone Mineral Density and Fracture Risk
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous loci associated with bone mineral density (BMD) and fracture risk, yet these variants explain only a fraction of the heritability. The 'missing heritability' may be partly explained by complex gene-gene (GxG) and gene-environment (GxE) interactions. Furthermore, as highlighted by Ioannidis's work on challenging phenotype definitions for vertebral fractures, environmental factors like smoking, nutrition, and physical activity likely modify genetic risk, but these interactions are poorly characterized at a genome-wide scale, limiting personalized risk prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-additive interactions between established BMD-associated genetic variants, and between these variants and modifiable environmental factors, significantly influence BMD and fracture risk, accounting for a substantial portion of the unexplained variance in susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-stage interaction analysis using data from large consortia (e.g., GEFOS, UK Biobank). First, for GxG, we will apply a targeted epistasis screening (using methods like BOOST or PLINK's epistasis tests) among SNPs from known BMD loci (e.g., 16q24). Second, for GxE, we will conduct genome-wide environment interaction studies (GWIS) using regression models with interaction terms for key environmental exposures (smoking, calcium intake, physical activity), adjusting for population stratification. Significance will be assessed using stringent, pre-registered multiplicity corrections (e.g., Bonferroni for GxG, false discovery rate for GWIS).
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond single-variant associations to map the interactive landscape of BMD genetics. It will quantify the collective contribution of GxG and GxE to fracture risk, identify specific high-risk genetic profiles contingent on environment, and provide a more nuanced biological model of osteoporosis etiology. The findings will inform targeted prevention strategies for individuals with specific genetic backgrounds.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined main genetic effects on BMD, a systematic, large-scale investigation of both GxG among known loci and genome-wide GxE for fracture-related phenotypes, using rigorous methods for high-dimensional interaction testing and pre-registered analytical protocols to mitigate false positives, remains novel.

proposals.title: A Multi-Ancestry GWAS Meta-Analysis for Osteoporotic Fracture Risk: Expanding Discovery and Quantifying Portability Gaps in Underrepresented Populations
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for complex traits like osteoporotic fracture are overwhelmingly based on European and, to a lesser extent, East Asian ancestries, severely limiting the generalizability of genetic risk scores and biological insights. This Eurocentric bias, a major concern in Ioannidis's work on population differences, obscures the true genetic architecture of disease in diverse groups and risks exacerbating health disparities. Extending rigorous GWAS to underrepresented ancestries (e.g., African, Hispanic, South Asian) is a critical step towards equitable genomic medicine and more robust causal inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conducting large-scale fracture GWAS in ancestries currently underrepresented in genomics will identify novel, population-specific risk loci and that the portability of polygenic risk scores (PRS) derived from European data will be significantly attenuated in these populations, reflecting differences in allele frequencies, linkage disequilibrium, and effect sizes.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-cohort meta-analysis of GWAS for incident osteoporotic fracture (hip, vertebral, major osteoporotic) in cohorts of African, Hispanic, and South Asian ancestry, harmonizing phenotype definitions as in Ioannidis's prior work on challenging fracture phenotypes. Analysis will use standard pre-2026 tools: association testing per cohort via REGENIE or SAIGE (accounting for relatedness), followed by fixed-effects inverse-variance-weighted meta-analysis using METAL. We will then assess cross-ancestry genetic correlation using LD Score regression and evaluate PRS portability by constructing scores from European summary statistics and testing their association and calibration in held-out target populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first well-powered fracture GWAS summary statistics for key underrepresented ancestries, directly addressing a critical evidence gap. It will quantify the performance penalty of applying Eurocentric PRS globally, providing empirical data to guide clinical translation. The discovery of novel loci will improve biological understanding of bone fragility across human populations.
proposals.novelty: While the need for diversity in genomics is recognized, this proposal provides a concrete, methodologically rigorous plan to execute it for a high-burden outcome (fracture) where non-European data is especially sparse, moving beyond commentary to actionable analysis. It uniquely integrates Ioannidis's expertise in fracture phenotype definition, cross-population genetic analysis, and the assessment of potential harm from non-portable associations.

proposals.title: Causal triangulation of bone mineral density, modifiable risk factors, and fracture risk: A multi-trait Mendelian randomization study
proposals.motivation: Osteoporotic fractures are a major public health burden, but the causal roles of many modifiable risk factors (e.g., physical activity, vitamin D, smoking) remain debated due to confounding and reverse causation in observational studies. While low bone mineral density (BMD) is a strong risk factor, a comprehensive causal framework integrating genetic instruments for BMD, these modifiable exposures, and fracture outcomes is lacking. Establishing such causality is essential for prioritizing effective public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) genetically proxied lower BMD has a direct causal effect on fracture risk, and (2) the effects of key modifiable risk factors on fracture are partially, but not fully, mediated through their causal effects on BMD.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-step, two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) analysis using summary statistics from large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for BMD, fracture, and selected modifiable factors (e.g., physical activity, serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, smoking initiation, BMI). We will use inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary method, supplemented by MR-Egger, weighted median, and MR-PRESSO to assess and correct for pleiotropy. We will then apply multivariable MR to estimate the direct effect of each exposure on fracture, conditional on BMD, and mediation analysis to quantify the proportion of effect mediated through BMD.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, genetically informed causal map of the pathways to fracture, distinguishing direct effects of lifestyle factors from those mediated via BMD. It will clarify which risk factors are likely to be the most effective intervention targets. The study will also serve as a methodological case study in applying triangulation and mediation frameworks within MR to a complex clinical endpoint.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been applied to BMD and fracture, a systematic causal triangulation integrating multiple modifiable exposures and formally testing for mediation through BMD is novel. This approach moves beyond estimating isolated effects to modeling the causal network, directly addressing the complexity highlighted in prior work on challenging fracture phenotypes.

proposals.title: Bayesian fine-mapping of fracture-associated loci to distinguish causal variants from tagging signals and assess cross-population portability
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for complex traits like fracture risk identify broad loci, but the specific causal variants remain elusive. This ambiguity limits biological insight and clinical translation, particularly for traits where genetic associations may be confounded by challenging phenotype definitions (e.g., radiographic vertebral fractures) or differ across populations. Fine-mapping is a critical next step to move from association to causality.
proposals.hypothesis: Within fracture-associated loci identified in prior GWAS, Bayesian fine-mapping will resolve a smaller set of high-probability causal variants, and the posterior inclusion probabilities of these variants will show significant heterogeneity across ancestries, reflecting differences in linkage disequilibrium and potentially causal allele frequency.
proposals.method: We will apply state-of-the-art Bayesian fine-mapping methods, specifically SuSiE (Sum of Single Effects) and FINEMAP, to existing large-scale GWAS summary statistics for fracture phenotypes. Analyses will be conducted within associated loci, conditioning on all variants in LD to compute posterior inclusion probabilities (PIPs) for causal variants. We will integrate multi-ancestry summary statistics to perform cross-population fine-mapping, assessing the consistency of causal signal localization and identifying population-specific credible sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a refined, high-confidence catalog of putative causal genetic variants for fracture risk, moving beyond tag SNPs. It will directly assess the portability of fine-mapped signals across populations, informing the genetic architecture of fracture risk and the design of functional validation studies. The project will also serve as a methodological case study for fine-mapping complex, clinically relevant binary traits.
proposals.novelty: While fine-mapping is established, its rigorous application to fracture genetics—a field challenged by phenotype heterogeneity—is limited. The novel integration of cross-population analysis directly tests the stability of causal inferences, a key concern for equitable translation of genetic findings, aligning with Ioannidis's interest in genetic contributions to disease incidence differences between groups.

proposals.title: Comparative Genetic Architecture of Extreme Bone Phenotypes: A Genome-Wide Association Study of High Bone Mineral Density (Sclerosteosis/Van Buchem Spectrum) versus Low BMD and Vertebral Fracture
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for low bone mineral density (BMD) and fracture risk have identified numerous common variants with small effects, but the genetic basis of extremely high BMD, as seen in sclerosteosis and Van Buchem disease, remains underexplored in population genetics. A systematic comparison of the genetic architecture at both extremes can elucidate whether high BMD is simply the inverse genetic spectrum of osteoporosis or involves distinct biological pathways. This has direct implications for understanding bone biology and for drug target discovery, where pathways causing high BMD (e.g., Wnt signaling inhibition) are already therapeutic targets for osteoporosis.
proposals.hypothesis: The polygenic architecture of extremely high BMD will show significant enrichment for variants in known Wnt signaling genes (e.g., SOST, LRP5) but will also be largely distinct from the polygenic risk score profile for low BMD and fracture, indicating that high BMD is not merely the inverse genetic risk of osteoporosis.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-stage GWAS. First, we will aggregate genetic data from individuals with extremely high BMD (e.g., >+4 SD), including carriers of rare SOST variants and population outliers, and compare them to large, existing GWAS consortia data for low BMD and radiographic vertebral fracture. We will use standard logistic regression models under an additive genetic model, adjusting for key covariates. Analyses will include: 1) Direct association testing at known loci, 2) Construction and comparison of polygenic risk scores (PRS) for low BMD applied to the high-BMD cohort, and 3) Genetic correlation analysis using LD score regression to quantify shared genetic etiology between the extreme phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative assessment of the genetic overlap between extreme high BMD and common osteoporosis. It will clarify whether therapeutic inhibition of pathways from monogenic high-BMD disorders is universally beneficial or could have unexpected risks in specific genetic backgrounds. The findings will refine the genetic risk prediction for fracture by identifying protective variants that may be missing from current PRS models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Ioannidis et al. has highlighted challenges in defining fracture phenotypes for GWAS, this proposal directly contrasts the genetic basis of a well-defined, extreme high-BMD phenotype against the low-BMD/fracture continuum, a focused 'natural experiment' not previously conducted at scale. It moves beyond single-variant associations to a systems-level comparison of polygenic architectures.

proposals.title: Correcting for Non-Independence in Cross-Population and Cross-Phenotype Genetic Association Meta-Analyses
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses of genome-wide association studies (GWAS) routinely assume participating cohorts are independent. This assumption is violated when studies share control groups or when population groups have substantial genetic overlap, as in admixed populations or across biobanks with participant overlap. Pre-2026, such non-independence can inflate test statistics, bias heritability estimates, and lead to false-positive cross-population comparisons, undermining the validity of risk assessment and genetic correlation analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the genetic covariance structure arising from shared controls and population overlap will yield more accurate estimates of genetic effect sizes, heritability, and cross-trait correlations, reducing false discovery rates in meta-analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate a statistical framework extending genetic covariance matrix-based methods (e.g., LD Score regression) to incorporate known sample overlap. The method will use individual-level genetic data from reference panels (e.g., 1000 Genomes) and summary statistics from large consortia (e.g., GEFOS for fracture, GIANT for obesity) to estimate the effective sample overlap. We will implement a modified inverse-variance weighted estimator that accounts for this covariance in meta-analysis, testing it via simulations and re-analysis of published cross-population GWAS for traits like vertebral fracture and obesity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for consortium-level analysis to produce less biased genetic association and correlation estimates. It will directly improve the rigor of research on genetic contributions to disease risk differences between populations and shared biology across traits, core themes in Ioannidis's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While methods exist for handling sample overlap within single studies, a generalizable framework for cross-study, cross-population meta-analysis that integrates estimated genetic correlation and explicit overlap correction is not standardized, addressing a critical gap in the reproducibility of genetic epidemiology.

proposals.title: A Data-Driven Framework for Study-Specific Weighting in Genetic Meta-Analysis to Improve Risk and Harm Estimation
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses of genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for complex traits like fracture risk and disease incidence rely heavily on user-specified weights, typically based on sample size. This approach ignores critical heterogeneity in study quality, phenotype definition (e.g., radiographic vs. clinical fractures), and population-specific genetic architecture, which can bias pooled effect estimates and mislead conclusions about genetic risk and potential harm. A systematic, quantitative method to derive study-specific weights is needed to improve the validity of meta-analytic findings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a weighting scheme incorporating quantitative metrics of study-specific data quality, phenotypic reliability, and genetic confounding will yield more accurate and replicable pooled estimates of genetic variant associations than standard inverse-variance weighting based on sample size alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-criteria weighting algorithm that calculates a composite weight for each study in a meta-analysis. Criteria will include: (1) a phenotype reliability score derived from internal consistency metrics and validation against gold-standard measures; (2) a genetic data quality score based on genotyping call rate, Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium p-values, and principal component analysis for population stratification; and (3) a population representativeness score adjusting for the study's contribution to trans-ethnic portability. We will test this framework using existing GWAS consortia data for vertebral fractures and obesity/nicotine addiction, comparing the stability and predictive performance of meta-analysis results under the new versus traditional weighting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transparent, reproducible toolkit for generating evidence-weighted meta-analyses in genetic epidemiology, moving beyond the simplistic assumption that sample size is the sole determinant of a study's informational value. It will directly address Ioannidis's focus on improving risk and harm estimation by reducing bias in pooled effect measures, particularly for challenging phenotypes and cross-population comparisons.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has noted the limitations of standard meta-analysis, no formal, generalizable framework exists to algorithmically integrate multiple dimensions of study-specific methodological rigor into the weighting process for genetic association studies, creating a bridge between methodological critique and practical implementation.

proposals.title: Bayesian Hierarchical Modeling of Heterogeneity in Genetic Association Studies of Fracture Risk: A Framework for Quantifying and Attributing Variability
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for complex traits like fracture risk often yield heterogeneous effect estimates across studies, complicating the interpretation of genetic risk. This heterogeneity, stemming from diverse populations, environmental exposures, and phenotypic definitions (e.g., radiographic vs. clinical fractures), is poorly quantified using standard meta-analytic tools. A rigorous, model-based approach is needed to dissect this heterogeneity, which is crucial for accurate risk prediction and understanding the biological basis of disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Bayesian hierarchical models can effectively partition the observed heterogeneity in fracture-related genetic associations into components attributable to study-level covariates (e.g., ancestry, age distribution, fracture definition) and residual variability, revealing that a significant portion of heterogeneity is systematic and explainable.
proposals.method: We will apply Bayesian random-effects meta-analysis models, implemented via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) in Stan or JAGS, to summary statistics from large-scale fracture GWAS consortia. The core model will treat genetic variant effect sizes as drawn from a distribution whose mean and variance are informed by study-level moderators. We will extend this to multivariate models for pleiotropic variants linked to both bone mineral density (BMD) and fracture, explicitly modeling the correlation structure. Model fit will be assessed using Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC) and posterior predictive checks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative framework for assessing and explaining heterogeneity in genetic epidemiology, moving beyond simple I² statistics. It will produce more nuanced estimates of genetic effect sizes for fracture risk and offer methodological guidance for integrating complex phenotypic definitions, such as those from Ioannidis's prior work on vertebral fractures, into genetic analyses.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian meta-analysis is established in clinical research, its application to dissect heterogeneity in genetic association studies, particularly for a challenging phenotype like fracture, is novel. The integration of genetic variant data with study-level moderators in a hierarchical Bayesian framework represents a significant methodological advance over standard fixed-effect or simple random-effects meta-analyses common in pre-2026 GWAS.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Genomic Bins of Null Association: A Framework for Defining Negative Controls and Informing Genetic Architecture
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) focus on identifying significant loci, but systematically characterizing genomic regions that consistently show no association across diverse traits and populations is neglected. Pre-2026, such 'null bins' could serve as robust empirical negative controls for calibration in Mendelian randomization and polygenic risk score analyses, addressing pervasive confounding from cryptic relatedness and population stratification. Furthermore, they could illuminate the genomic and functional constraints that make certain regions inert for complex disease risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-overlapping genomic bins (e.g., 1 Mb) can be identified which, across a wide array of independent GWAS for complex diseases and quantitative traits, consistently rank in the lowest percentiles of association statistics, and that these regions are enriched for specific functional and evolutionary features.
proposals.method: We will aggregate summary statistics from >100 large-scale, pre-2026 GWAS (e.g., UK Biobank, GIANT, GEFOS) for diverse traits. The genome will be partitioned into 1 Mb bins. For each bin and each study, we will compute a rank-based metric (e.g., the 90th percentile of -log10(p-values) of SNPs within it). Bins consistently in the bottom 5% across all studies will be identified. We will then perform enrichment analyses using pre-2026 functional annotations (e.g., ENCODE, chromatin states, sequence conservation) to characterize these 'null' regions versus genomic background and versus top-associated bins.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first curated map of consistently null genomic regions, providing a resource for empirical negative control selection in genetic epidemiology. It will contribute to understanding the genetic architecture of complex traits by defining what parts of the genome are not involved, potentially reflecting functional constraints or evolutionary neutrality for common variant associations.
proposals.novelty: While methods exist to find significant loci, a systematic, cross-trait hunt for consistent null regions is novel. This flips the standard GWAS paradigm, focusing on the absence of signal as a biologically and methodologically informative property, directly extending Ioannidis's work on challenging phenotypes and genetic contributions to population differences.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in the Timing of Intervention Effects: A Mendelian Randomization and Survival Analysis Study of High-Risk Genetic Subgroups for Fracture Prevention
proposals.motivation: Preventive interventions, such as osteoporosis drugs, are often studied in broad populations, potentially masking differential benefits in high-risk subgroups. This is critical for resource allocation and personalized medicine. Pre-2026 genetic epidemiology provides tools to define high-risk subgroups using polygenic risk scores (PRS), but the interaction between genetic risk and the timing of intervention benefit remains poorly quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals in the highest decile of genetic risk for osteoporotic fracture derive a significantly greater absolute risk reduction from early pharmacological intervention (e.g., bisphosphonate initiation) compared to the population average, and that this benefit is most pronounced when intervention occurs earlier in the disease course.
proposals.method: We will construct a PRS for radiographic vertebral fracture using the latest pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics, including the 16q24 locus. Using large biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank), we will apply Mendelian Randomization (MR) with genetic variants as proxies for early intervention, stratified by PRS deciles, to estimate subgroup-specific effects on fracture incidence. Time-to-event analyses (Cox models with time-varying coefficients) will test for interaction between genetic risk strata and the timing of intervention (age at initiation). Sensitivity analyses will assess pleiotropy and robustness of the PRS.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating timing effects in genetic high-risk subgroups, moving beyond average population effects. It will offer evidence for or against the prioritization of early intervention based on genetic profiling and inform the design of future stratified prevention trials.
proposals.novelty: While PRS identify high-risk groups, this study uniquely integrates MR with survival analysis to explicitly model how genetic risk modifies the effect of intervention *timing*, a key but understudied dimension of heterogeneous treatment effects.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Endotyping of Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: A Multi-Omics Study of Risk, Mechanisms, and Prognosis
proposals.motivation: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) is a severe complication of SLE with heterogeneous presentation and unpredictable course. Current management relies on broad immunosuppression, reflecting a lack of understanding of distinct pathogenic drivers. Pre-2026, genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and multi-omics have revealed disease heterogeneity in other conditions, but AIHA in SLE remains poorly stratified beyond serology, hindering personalized risk assessment and therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AIHA in SLE comprises mechanistically distinct endotypes, identifiable through integrated multi-omics data, which are associated with differential genetic risk, immune pathway activation, disease trajectories, and treatment responses.
proposals.method: We will prospectively collect genomic (GWAS array), transcriptomic (RNA-seq from peripheral blood mononuclear cells), proteomic (Olink or SomaScan panels), and deep clinical/phenotypic data from a discovery cohort of SLE patients with and without AIHA. Unsupervised clustering (e.g., Similarity Network Fusion) will integrate omics layers to define candidate endotypes. Associations with genetic variants (polygenic risk scores), pathway dysregulation (Gene Set Enrichment Analysis), and clinical outcomes (time-to-relapse, treatment failure) will be tested. Findings will be validated in an independent cohort using the same clustering model.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first biologically grounded taxonomy of SLE-associated AIHA, moving beyond descriptive clinical phenotypes. It will identify specific molecular pathways for targeted intervention and generate a prognostic framework to stratify patients by risk of severe outcomes or treatment resistance, directly informing trial design and management.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omics has been applied to SLE broadly, this is a focused, deep phenotyping study of a critical hematologic manifestation. The integration of genetic risk data with dynamic omics to define stable endotypes addresses a key gap between genetic association and mechanistic understanding in a complex disease complication.

proposals.title: Causal Inference at Single-Cell Resolution: A Mendelian Randomization Framework to Link LRRK2 Genetic Variation to Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia Risk via B Cell and Monocyte Dysregulation
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified the LRRK2 locus as a risk factor for several autoimmune diseases, including potential links to autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), but establishing causality and pinpointing the specific immune cell types and molecular pathways involved remains a major challenge. Traditional Mendelian randomization (MR) studies using bulk tissue expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) lack the resolution to identify the causal cellular contexts, limiting mechanistic insight and therapeutic target validation.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetic variants in the LRRK2 locus causally influence AIHA risk by altering LRRK2 expression and downstream signaling pathways specifically within B cells and monocytes, leading to dysregulated transcriptional programs that promote autoantibody production or aberrant erythrocyte clearance.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes (CITE-seq) on peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from a cohort genotyped for LRRK2 variants. We will first map cis-expression quantitative trait loci (cis-eQTLs) for LRRK2 at single-cell resolution, identifying variant effects on expression within specific immune cell subsets. Using these genetic variants as instrumental variables, we will then implement a single-cell MR framework, applying Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) regression and inverse-variance weighted (IVW) methods to estimate the causal effect of LRRK2 expression on cell-type-specific gene programs (e.g., interferon response, B cell activation) and surface protein markers linked to AIHA pathology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, causal evidence linking a non-Mendelian autoimmune disease risk locus to dysregulated molecular pathways within specific human immune cells, moving beyond association to mechanism. It will establish a novel analytical framework that fuses single-cell genomics with MR principles, offering a generalizable template for dissecting the cellular etiology of complex genetic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While MR is established for population-level inference and single-cell eQTLs are being mapped, the explicit integration of genetic variants as instruments within a single-cell experimental system to perform causal pathway analysis is a novel methodological fusion. This directly tests the core MR assumption of the instrument affecting the outcome only through the exposure within the relevant cellular context.

proposals.title: A Network Meta-Analysis of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus-Related Autoantibodies and Their Relative Association with Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia Risk
proposals.motivation: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) is a severe complication of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), but the relative contribution of specific autoantibodies (e.g., anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-Ro/SSA) to AIHA risk remains poorly quantified. Existing pairwise meta-analyses are limited, preventing a unified ranking of risk factors. This gap hinders risk stratification and understanding of pathogenic mechanisms. A comparative synthesis of evidence is needed to inform clinical monitoring and mechanistic studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a network meta-analysis will reveal a hierarchy of association strengths between specific SLE-related autoantibodies and AIHA, with anti-dsDNA and anti-phospholipid antibodies demonstrating the strongest relative associations compared to other autoantibody specificities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic literature review to identify all observational studies (case-control, cross-sectional, cohort) reporting associations between SLE-related autoantibodies and AIHA. Using Bayesian network meta-analysis (NMA) with random-effects models, we will synthesize direct and indirect evidence to estimate relative odds ratios for AIHA for each autoantibody. We will employ Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods via software like WinBUGS or R's 'gemtc' package, assessing consistency, heterogeneity, and publication bias using node-splitting and comparison-adjusted funnel plots.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative ranking of SLE autoantibodies by their association with AIHA, moving beyond pairwise comparisons. It will offer clinicians a evidence-based hierarchy for risk assessment in SLE patients. Furthermore, it will generate hypotheses about distinct biological pathways linking specific autoimmunity to erythrocyte destruction, guiding future genetic and mechanistic research.
proposals.novelty: While NMA is established for comparing treatments, its application to compare multiple disease biomarkers for a specific complication is novel in autoimmunity. This approach directly addresses the challenge of synthesizing fragmented evidence on multiple correlated risk factors, a methodological gap in etiological research highlighted by Ioannidis's work on challenging phenotypes and risk estimation.

proposals.title: Anchoring Statistical Phasing with Molecular Haplotypes to Refine Protective Allele Associations in Complex Disease
proposals.motivation: Statistical phasing of genetic variants, while powerful, introduces errors that can obscure true haplotype-disease associations, leading to false-positive or false-negative findings in genetic epidemiology. This is particularly problematic for identifying protective haplotypes, where accurate phase is critical for defining the specific allelic combination conferring reduced risk. Such errors undermine the reliability of genetic risk prediction and the biological interpretation of GWAS loci.
proposals.hypothesis: Using long-read sequencing to establish a high-confidence molecular haplotype scaffold in a subset of a study population will significantly improve the accuracy of statistical phasing for the entire cohort, leading to the refined identification of true protective haplotypes associated with complex diseases like osteoporosis.
proposals.method: We will select a diverse subset (n=100) from a large osteoporosis cohort with existing GWAS data. These individuals will undergo whole-genome sequencing using PacBio HiFi for long-read molecular haplotyping. The resulting high-confidence haplotype maps will serve as anchors. We will then apply and iteratively correct leading statistical phasing algorithms (e.g., SHAPEIT4, Eagle2) on the full cohort (n>10,000), using the anchor haplotypes for validation and error correction via a custom integration pipeline. The refined haplotypes will be tested for association with radiographic vertebral fracture risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust methodological framework for integrating empirical and statistical phasing to minimize haplotype error. It will deliver a more accurate map of protective haplotype structures for complex traits, directly enhancing the translational potential of genetic findings for risk prediction and drug target identification.
proposals.novelty: While long-read sequencing is used for *de novo* assembly, its systematic use as an empirical anchor to correct and validate statistical phasing in large-scale association studies is novel. This hybrid approach directly targets the core limitation of statistical inference with empirical gold-standard data.

proposals.title: Niche Conservatism of a Protective Haplotype: Testing Effect Portability Across Ancestries Using Ecological Niche Modeling Principles
proposals.motivation: The portability of genetic risk estimates across populations is a major challenge for genomic medicine and equitable risk prediction. Pre-2026 GWAS have shown that effect sizes of disease-associated variants can differ across ancestries, but methods to formally model and predict this variation are limited. This lack of predictive models hinders the assessment of whether a 'protective' variant identified in one population will confer the same benefit in another, risking miscalibrated interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The protective effect of a haplotype is not uniform but is a function of its local genetic background; therefore, a model of this effect built in one ancestry will fail to accurately predict the effect size when projected to a genetically distinct population, analogous to a species' niche model failing in a new environment.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MaxEnt (Maximum Entropy) algorithm, a core ecological niche modeling (ENM) method, to model the haplotype's effect size as a function of the frequencies of interacting alleles and background polygenic risk scores in a source population (e.g., European-ancestry UK Biobank data). This statistical 'niche model' will then be projected onto the allele frequency distributions of target populations (e.g., African, East Asian ancestries in All of Us or Biobank Japan). Predicted effect sizes will be compared to observed effects in validation cohorts using meta-regression, with systematic deviations indicating non-conservatism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for testing the portability of genetic effects, moving beyond simple comparisons of effect sizes. It will directly inform the generalizability of genetic findings and polygenic risk scores, highlighting specific genetic background factors that modulate protective effects. This contributes to more rigorous and equitable translation of genetic discoveries.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual novelty lies in applying the formal framework of ecological niche conservatism—a well-established theory in biogeography—to the problem of genetic effect portability, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously explored. Methodologically, it repurposes a mature ENM algorithm (MaxEnt) for a novel genetic prediction task.

proposals.title: Uniformity of a Protective Haplotype Effect on Fracture Risk Across Diverse Genetic Ancestries: A Multi-Cohort Meta-Analysis
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified genetic variants and haplotypes associated with disease risk, but their effect sizes are often estimated in populations of European ancestry. This raises critical questions about the transferability and uniformity of these effects across ancestries, which is essential for equitable genetic risk prediction and understanding the fundamental biology of disease. Testing the consistency of a protective haplotype's effect, such as one linked to bone mineral density and fracture risk, across diverse genetic backgrounds addresses a key gap in genetic epidemiology and the generalizability of findings.
proposals.hypothesis: The protective effect size of a specific haplotype (e.g., the 16q24 BMD locus implicated in vertebral fracture risk) on the relevant disease outcome is uniform and does not vary significantly across major human genetic ancestries.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-ancestry meta-analysis of individual-level genetic and phenotypic data from large biobanks and fracture consortia (e.g., UK Biobank, GEFOS, Million Veteran Program). The analysis will focus on a pre-specified protective haplotype. We will use mixed-effects meta-regression models, with genetic ancestry (via principal components) as a moderator, to formally test for heterogeneity in the haplotype's odds ratio for fracture across groups. Methods will include ancestry-specific association testing using logistic regression adjusted for covariates, followed by Cochran's Q and I² statistics for heterogeneity, and Bayesian meta-analytic approaches to estimate the posterior probability of effect uniformity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on whether a key genetic protective factor has a consistent biological effect across populations, which is crucial for validating its causal role. It will directly inform the portability of polygenic risk scores for fractures and contribute to the broader discussion on reducing bias in genetic research by moving beyond single-ancestry studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Ioannidis and others has highlighted challenges in phenotype definition and cross-population differences in genetic architecture, this proposal explicitly tests the uniformity of a specific, putatively protective haplotype effect—a focused question that moves beyond cataloging differences to assessing fundamental biological consistency.

proposals.title: Detecting High-Impact Rare Variants in Complex Traits: A Model-Based Pattern Recognition Approach Adapted from Astrophysics
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have been highly successful for common variants but struggle to identify rare variants with potentially large effect sizes, as standard frequency-based filters and burden tests discard or dilute these signals. This is analogous to the challenge in astrophysics of finding rare, significant objects like Earth-like exoplanets against a noisy background. Developing methods to systematically detect these 'needles in the haystack' is crucial for explaining missing heritability and identifying high-risk genetic factors for diseases like osteoporosis, where rare variants in genes like LRP5 have demonstrated outsized effects on fracture risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Rare genetic variants with clinically significant effects can be reliably identified by their multi-dimensional signature, distinct from common variant signals, using pattern-recognition algorithms adapted from rare signal detection in astrophysics.
proposals.method: We will adapt model-based detection algorithms, such as the Matched Filter technique used to find exoplanets in stellar light curves, to genomic data. The algorithm will be trained on a multi-dimensional signature template comprising features like: 1) familial segregation patterns from pedigree data, 2) functional genomic annotations (e.g., CADD scores, conservation), 3) extreme phenotypic values in carriers, and 4) co-localization with known disease-associated loci. We will apply this to whole-genome sequencing data from large cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank) for traits like radiographic vertebral fracture, scanning for variants that match this signature despite low allele frequency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for rare variant discovery that moves beyond frequency-based heuristics, potentially identifying new high-effect risk alleles. It will contribute a methodologically transparent tool for the field, addressing Ioannidis's documented interest in improving genetic association analysis and phenotype definition for complex traits like fracture.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual and technical translation of rare signal detection algorithms from astrophysics to genomics is novel, offering a model-based alternative to existing statistical aggregation tests that often lack a specific signal model for high-impact rare variants.

proposals.title: The Inverse Frequency Fallacy: A Quantitative Assessment of Clinical Effect Sizes in Rare Variants Missed by Population-Based Genetic Association Studies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genetic epidemiology is dominated by large-scale GWAS and population-based association studies, which often filter out or underpower analyses for rare variants (MAF < 0.01). This creates a potential blind spot: the assumption that allele frequency is inversely correlated with clinical effect size may be flawed, leading to the systematic omission of rare variants with strong, clinically actionable effects on disease risk and harm. This is critical for complex traits like fracture risk, where rare coding variants in genes like SOST have shown large effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of genetic variants with large effect sizes (odds ratios > 3.0) on clinically relevant binary outcomes (e.g., radiographic vertebral fracture) are rare (MAF < 0.01) and are systematically missed or misclassified in standard frequency-first association studies, leading to an overestimation of the predictive power of common polygenic risk scores.
proposals.method: We will perform a focused re-analysis of existing whole-exome and whole-genome sequencing data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) and fracture-specific cohorts. Using a case-control design, we will employ burden tests and sequence kernel association tests (SKAT/SKAT-O) to aggregate rare variants within genes and pathways. We will then compare the effect size distribution of significant rare variant signals against the top common variant signals from GWAS for the same phenotype, using metrics like the population attributable fraction and the genetic variance explained.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating the 'inverse frequency fallacy' in genetic association studies. It will generate an evidence-based estimate of the clinical relevance buried in the rare variant spectrum for fracture risk, directly informing future study design and variant prioritization for clinical translation. It will challenge the implicit prioritization of common variants in risk prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of rare variants with large effects is known, a systematic, quantitative assessment of their collective clinical impact relative to the common variant paradigm—specifically testing the assumption that frequency directly indicates relevance—is lacking. This proposal directly confronts a foundational, often unstated, filter in mainstream genetic analysis.

proposals.title: Environmental Modifiers of Penetrance for LRRK2 Genetic Variants: A Multi-Cohort Interaction Analysis
proposals.motivation: The LRRK2 G2019S mutation is a major genetic risk factor for Parkinson's disease (PD), yet its penetrance is incomplete and variable, suggesting environmental modifiers. Understanding this gene-environment interaction is critical for accurate risk prediction and for identifying modifiable protective factors. This addresses a core challenge in genetic epidemiology: moving from static genetic associations to dynamic models of disease expression.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific environmental exposures, such as tobacco use, caffeine intake, pesticide exposure, and head injury, significantly modify the penetrance of both risk-increasing (e.g., G2019S) and putative protective LRRK2 variants for Parkinson's disease.
proposals.method: We will perform a coordinated meta-analysis of individual-level genetic and environmental data from multiple large PD consortia (e.g., IPDGC). For each pre-specified environmental factor, we will fit logistic regression models with PD status as outcome, including LRRK2 variant dosage, environmental exposure, and their multiplicative interaction term, adjusted for age, sex, and principal components. Penetrance will be estimated using a modified Kaplan-Meier analysis for carriers. Sensitivity analyses will assess robustness to measurement error and unmeasured confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify, for the first time in a large-scale framework, how specific lifestyles and exposures alter the risk conferred by a major PD gene. It will provide empirical evidence for or against the static nature of genetic risk, directly informing personalized prevention strategies for LRRK2 carriers and refining etiological models of PD.
proposals.novelty: While LRRK2-environment interactions have been suggested in small studies, this large-scale, systematic test across multiple cohorts and exposures is novel. It applies rigorous interaction methodology, championed by Ioannidis for assessing genetic effects across populations, to the critical question of penetrance modification within a high-impact locus.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Clinical Impact of Novel Genetic Variants via Multi-Parametric Phenotype Niche Modeling
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and sequencing efforts routinely identify novel variants of uncertain significance (VUS), but their clinical relevance remains ambiguous. Existing statistical and functional assays often fail to capture the complex, polygenic context in which these variants operate, leading to unreliable risk estimates and potential harm from misclassification. A method to quantify a variant's impact within a realistic, multi-factorial biological system is critically needed for translational genetics.
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical relevance of novel genetic variants (e.g., p.A419V, p.M1646T) can be calibrated by measuring their marginal contribution to the predictive accuracy of a comprehensive, multi-omic model of disease phenotype, analogous to assessing a new environmental factor's impact on an ecological niche model.
proposals.method: We will construct a 'clinical phenotype niche' model using pre-2026 machine learning methods (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting) trained on population-scale genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic data from established cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank) with longitudinal outcomes like fracture incidence or disease progression. The baseline model will include known risk variants and covariates. We will then introduce the novel variants as additional features and rigorously quantify the change in model performance (e.g., C-index, net reclassification improvement) using nested cross-validation. Sensitivity analyses will assess robustness to model specification and population stratification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, context-aware framework for calibrating the functional impact of VUS, moving beyond simple association metrics. It will deliver a quantitative estimate of clinical relevance for the specific variants studied and establish a generalizable methodology for future variant interpretation. This directly addresses Ioannidis's focus on rigorous risk assessment and the challenge of translating genetic findings to clinically meaningful effects.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual adaptation of ecological niche modeling principles to clinical genetics and its operational focus on model calibration rather than mere discovery. It integrates the variant into a pre-existing, complex system model to measure its perturbative effect, a non-obvious shift from standard additive or isolated functional assays.

proposals.title: Phenotypic Characterization and Clinical Risk Assessment of the p.A419V and p.M1646T Genetic Variants in Large-Scale Biobank Data
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous genetic variants associated with disease risk, but their clinical relevance often remains unquantified. The specific missense variants p.A419V and p.M1646T have been flagged as potentially consequential, yet their impact on disease onset, progression, and phenotypic severity in real-world populations is unknown. Determining whether these associations translate to clinically meaningful effects is a critical step for moving from statistical genetics to actionable medical insight, addressing a key gap in the post-GWAS era.
proposals.hypothesis: The p.A419V and p.M1646T variants are associated not only with binary disease risk but also with measurable differences in disease onset age, progression trajectories, and specific phenotypic sub-manifestations in relevant clinical populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comprehensive phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) for the two variants using harmonized data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, FinnGen). We will employ Mendelian Randomization (MR) analyses, using the variants as instrumental variables, to estimate their potential causal effects on disease progression metrics. For time-to-event outcomes (e.g., fracture, disease complication), we will apply Cox proportional hazards models, stratifying by genotype and adjusting for key covariates. Phenotypic severity will be assessed using electronic health record-derived quantitative traits and disease-specific severity scores where available.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, multi-dimensional assessment of the clinical relevance of two novel risk variants, moving beyond simple odds ratios. It will establish a framework for prioritizing variants for further mechanistic study or potential clinical translation. The findings will directly inform the ongoing debate about the utility of genetic risk scores and the interpretation of genetic associations for complex diseases.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified these variants, this proposal systematically investigates their influence on *dynamic* clinical outcomes (onset, progression) rather than static case-control status. It integrates PheWAS, MR, and time-to-event analysis specifically to dissect clinical relevance, a direct application of Ioannidis's focus on moving from genetic association to understanding real-world harm and benefit.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Subgroup Discovery in Parkinson's Disease: An Unsupervised Multi-Omic Integration Framework for Clinical Trial Stratification
proposals.motivation: The high failure rate of Parkinson's disease (PD) clinical trials is partly attributable to patient heterogeneity, where broad clinical diagnoses encompass diverse biological mechanisms. Pre-2026, trials targeting single genetic mutations (e.g., LRRK2, GBA) are necessarily exclusive, while trials in idiopathic PD risk being underpowered due to unmeasured biological variation. A data-driven, mechanism-based stratification framework is urgently needed to create more homogeneous and inclusive trial cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating polygenic risk scores with deep molecular phenotyping will reveal biologically distinct patient subgroups that are more predictive of therapeutic response than clinical diagnosis alone, and these subgroups can be identified using unsupervised machine learning on multi-modal data.
proposals.method: We will integrate data from existing cohorts (e.g., PPMI, UK Biobank) including genome-wide genotyping (for polygenic risk score calculation), CSF proteomics (Olink/SOMAscan panels), structural/functional neuroimaging, and digital motor assessments. We will apply an unsupervised multi-view clustering pipeline, using methods like Similarity Network Fusion (SNF) or Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA), to identify patient clusters. Cluster stability will be assessed via bootstrapping, and biological pathway enrichment (e.g., lysosomal, inflammatory, synaptic) will be characterized for each subgroup.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for defining PD patient strata based on shared biology rather than single mutations or coarse clinical features. It will generate testable hypotheses about which therapeutic mechanisms (e.g., alpha-synuclein reduction, GCase enhancement) are most relevant for each subgroup, directly informing the design of future precision medicine trials.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omic studies exist, the explicit application of unsupervised integration to define adaptive, mechanism-based subgroups for clinical trial enrichment in PD is novel. This moves beyond single-variant genetic risk to a holistic, polygenic and phenotypic risk model for trial design.

proposals.title: A Composite LRRK2 Risk Score (CLRS) for Parkinson's Disease: Translating Ecological Risk Assessment Methodology to Genetic Locus Aggregation
proposals.motivation: The LRRK2 locus is a major genetic contributor to Parkinson's disease (PD), but risk is influenced by a heterogeneous mix of coding variants (e.g., G2019S), non-coding variants, and potential epistatic interactions. Traditional polygenic risk scores (PRS) often treat variants additively and may fail to capture this complexity, limiting predictive power for disease risk and phenotypic heterogeneity. A novel aggregation method is needed to integrate these diverse genetic 'stressors' into a unified, biologically informed metric.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Composite LRRK2 Risk Score (CLRS), constructed using multivariate weighting and interaction models adapted from ecological Composite Environmental Risk Scores (CERS), will outperform standard additive PRS in predicting PD case-control status and explain a greater proportion of phenotypic variance among carriers.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CERS framework, which uses multivariate statistical models (e.g., principal component analysis, structural equation modeling) to integrate heterogeneous environmental stressors. For the CLRS, we will aggregate all common and rare LRRK2-region variants from large-scale genetics consortia (e.g., IPDGC). Instead of simple effect-size weighting, we will employ ecological model structures to weight variants based on their independent contribution and modeled interactions within the locus. The score's predictive performance will be rigorously tested for association with PD risk and progression metrics, with careful calibration of false-positive risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more nuanced tool for risk prediction specific to the most important PD locus, moving beyond additive PRS. It will offer a methodological template for aggregating complex genetic signals at other pleiotropic loci. The results could improve genetic counseling for LRRK2 variant carriers and inform the stratification of patients for targeted therapies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary translation of a well-established ecological risk assessment framework to a human genetics problem, explicitly modeling intra-locus variant interactions in a way standard PRS methodologies do not.

proposals.title: Mendelian Randomization Analysis of LRRK2 Variants: Causal Inference for Parkinson's Disease Risk and Related Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: Variants in the LRRK2 gene are strongly associated with Parkinson's disease (PD), but observational studies cannot reliably distinguish causation from confounding or reverse causality for associated risk factors and disease modifiers. Establishing causality is critical for prioritizing therapeutic targets and understanding disease mechanisms. Mendelian randomization (MR) provides a powerful, genetically-instrumented framework to infer causality, but its rigorous application to the LRRK2 locus for PD and related traits is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetically predicted variation in LRRK2 function, instrumented by specific non-pathogenic variants, has a causal effect on PD risk and may also influence related phenotypes (e.g., inflammatory markers, specific cancer risks) previously linked in observational epidemiology, thereby clarifying the causal pathway.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-sample MR design using summary statistics from large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for PD and candidate secondary traits. Genetic instruments will be carefully selected LRRK2 region variants (e.g., rs34637584, rs1491942) that are strongly associated with LRRK2 expression or function but not with the primary GWAS outcome through linkage disequilibrium with known pathogenic mutations. We will apply inverse-variance weighted, MR-Egger, and weighted median methods to estimate causal effects, with sensitivity analyses for pleiotropy using MR-PRESSO and Steiger filtering for directionality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, genetically-validated evidence for or against a causal role of LRRK2 in PD etiology, moving beyond association. It will also systematically test causal links between LRRK2 and other health outcomes, informing the potential risks and mechanisms of LRRK2-targeted therapies. The analysis will serve as a methodological case study in applying MR to a high-profile locus with complex genetic architecture.
proposals.novelty: While MR is widely used, a focused, rigorous MR analysis specifically for the LRRK2 locus, accounting for its unique linkage disequilibrium structure and potential pleiotropic effects, is novel. This approach moves past simple genetic association to formally test causality for a key gene in neurodegeneration.

proposals.title: Controlling for Population Stratification in LRRK2 Association Studies Using a Pedigree-Based Animal Model Adapted from Conservation Genetics
proposals.motivation: Population stratification remains a critical confounder in genetic association studies, particularly for risk loci like LRRK2 in Parkinson's disease, where spurious associations can arise from allele frequency differences between subpopulations. Standard methods like principal component analysis may be insufficient in admixed or structured populations. Conservation genetics has long addressed the analogous problem of estimating genetic parameters in wild, structured populations using pedigree-based models, offering a robust but untapped framework for human genetics.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the pedigree-based 'animal model' from quantitative genetics will effectively control for population stratification in LRRK2 studies, providing less biased estimates of LRRK2 effect sizes and heritability for related quantitative endophenotypes compared to family-based association tests (FBATs) and standard case-control approaches.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of individuals with known familial relationships, including extended pedigrees, genotyped for LRRK2 variants and genome-wide markers. We will construct a genomic relationship matrix (GRM) from the pedigree and/or genome-wide data. Using a linear mixed model framework (the animal model), we will partition phenotypic variance (e.g., quantitative motor scores or LRRK2 kinase activity) into additive genetic variance components attributable to the LRRK2 locus, polygenic background (via the GRM), and residual error. Association testing for the LRRK2 variant will be performed within this model using a likelihood ratio test.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more powerful and statistically robust method for LRRK2 association analysis, particularly for quantitative traits, by explicitly modeling relatedness. It will yield accurate estimates of the LRRK2 variant's heritability and effect size, controlling for stratification without requiring assumptions of population homogeneity. The approach will demonstrate the utility of cross-disciplinary methodological transfer from conservation to human genetics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of the pedigree-based 'animal model,' a cornerstone of evolutionary and conservation genetics, to solve a persistent confounding problem in human complex trait genetics, moving beyond standard FBATs or PCA-adjusted models.

proposals.title: Family-Based Association Testing of LRRK2 Variants and Complex Disease Risk: Controlling for Population Stratification to Refine Genetic Effect Estimates
proposals.motivation: Population stratification is a major confounder in genetic association studies, potentially leading to spurious findings and inflated effect sizes, a critical concern in meta-research on risk and harm. The LRRK2 gene is a prime example, with variants implicated in Parkinson's disease and other complex traits, but reported associations may be confounded by ancestry. Applying robust methods to control for this is essential for accurate risk estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying family-based association test (FBAT) designs to LRRK2 variant data will yield more conservative and less confounded estimates of genetic effect sizes on disease risk compared to standard case-control designs, by inherently controlling for population stratification.
proposals.method: We will apply the Family-Based Association Test (FBAT) framework, specifically the FBAT and FBAT-GEE algorithms, to existing genetic datasets containing LRRK2 variants and relevant disease phenotypes (e.g., Parkinson's disease, Crohn's disease). The analysis will use the transmission disequilibrium test (TDT) principle within families to assess association, which is robust to population admixture. We will compare effect estimates (odds ratios) and their significance from FBAT analyses with those derived from conventional case-control analyses performed on the same datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, stratification-free assessment of LRRK2's role in complex diseases, directly addressing concerns about confounding in genetic epidemiology. It will contribute a methodological case study on the value of family-based designs for validating genetic associations and refining risk estimates, a core theme in Ioannidis's work on reducing bias.
proposals.novelty: While FBAT is a known method, its systematic application to dissect potential confounding in the widely studied LRRK2 locus, specifically to quantify the difference in effect estimates between study designs, is novel and addresses a persistent gap in translating genetic associations to reliable risk metrics.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Genetic Variant Pathogenicity from Population Allele Frequency and Phenotypic Association Spectra
proposals.motivation: Current methods for predicting variant pathogenicity rely heavily on evolutionary conservation and molecular features, often failing to integrate population-scale epidemiological signatures. This limits clinical interpretation, especially for variants of uncertain significance (VUS) in diverse populations. An approach that systematically inverts population genetic and phenotypic association data to infer pathogenic mechanism could significantly improve risk assessment and variant prioritization.
proposals.hypothesis: The joint distribution of a genetic variant's allele frequency across global populations and its association profile with a spectrum of clinical phenotypes contains sufficient information to infer its underlying pathogenic characteristics, including its mode of inheritance, penetrance, and selective pressure.
proposals.method: We will develop a forward model that simulates, for a given set of variant parameters (e.g., selection coefficient, dominance coefficient), the expected allele frequency spectrum across 1000 Genomes populations and the association Z-score profile across UK Biobank disease endpoints. We will then implement a Bayesian inference framework, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, to invert observed data from public repositories (gnomAD, GWAS Catalog) and estimate the posterior distribution of the variant's pathogenic parameters. Model calibration will use known pathogenic and benign variants from ClinVar.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrative computational framework for variant interpretation that quantitatively links population genetics and phenotypic associations. It will provide a principled method for generating mechanistic hypotheses for VUS and improve the assessment of genetic risk across ancestrally diverse groups.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly framing variant interpretation as a formal inverse problem, analogous to seismology, and in jointly modeling population and complex phenotype signatures within a single Bayesian inference engine to deduce pathogenic mechanism.

proposals.title: Inferring Variant Pathogenicity from Population Allele Frequencies and Disease Profiles: A Bayesian Framework for Risk Calibration
proposals.motivation: Current methods for predicting variant pathogenicity (e.g., PolyPhen-2, CADD) rely heavily on evolutionary conservation and molecular features, often lacking direct calibration to observed disease risk in human populations. This can lead to misclassification, especially for variants with moderate effects or those influencing complex traits like fracture risk. A method that systematically integrates population-scale allele frequency data and disease association profiles could provide more epidemiologically grounded pathogenicity estimates, addressing a key challenge in post-GWAS functional interpretation.
proposals.hypothesis: The pathogenicity of a genetic variant can be probabilistically inferred by modeling the joint distribution of its allele frequency across diverse populations and its association profile across a spectrum of related diseases, under constraints of population genetics and known disease architectures.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where the prior for a variant's effect size distribution is informed by its global and ancestry-specific allele frequencies, incorporating purifying selection constraints. The likelihood will be based on large-scale, pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics for a curated set of related disease phenotypes (e.g., vertebral fracture, BMD, obesity, nicotine addiction).
