We will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, specifically Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan, to estimate posterior probabilities of pathogenicity for each variant, validating against established clinical variant databases (ClinVar) and simulated data where ground truth is known.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, calibrated metric for variant pathogenicity that directly links population genetic data to disease risk profiles. It will provide a framework for assessing the contribution of genetic variants to differences in disease incidence between population groups, a key interest in Ioannidis's recent work. The method will be applied to loci associated with fracture risk and metabolic traits, offering new insights into their biological basis and potential for harm.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools use frequency as a filter, our approach formally inverts the problem, using frequency and multi-trait association profiles as primary inputs to *estimate* pathogenicity, creating a bridge between population genetics and clinical prediction. This contrasts with methods that primarily use sequence-based features or single-disease associations.

proposals.title: Disentangling Resilience from Delayed Onset: A Prospective Cohort Study of High-Penetrance Variant Carriers to Identify Protective Genetic and Environmental Modifiers
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and sequencing efforts have identified high-penetrance variants for diseases like breast cancer (BRCA1/2) or neurodegenerative disorders, yet not all carriers develop disease, suggesting protective factors. However, existing cross-sectional studies of 'unaffected carriers' are confounded by the possibility of future delayed onset, inflating estimates of resilience and obscuring true protective mechanisms. A prospective design with stringent baseline phenotyping and long-term follow-up is required to definitively distinguish lifelong resilience from delayed onset.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals carrying high-penetrance risk variants who remain disease-free for a prolonged period after the typical age of onset possess distinct genetic, epigenetic, and environmental profiles that confer true biological protection, which are differentiable from the profiles of carriers who will later develop the disease.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective cohort of 2,000 disease-free carriers of high-penetrance variants (e.g., in BRCA1, BRCA2, or Mendelian disease genes) confirmed via clinical exam, imaging, and biomarkers. Participants will undergo deep phenotyping at baseline and biennially. We will employ whole-genome sequencing, methylation arrays (Illumina EPIC), and plasma proteomics (Olink/SomaScan) to generate multi-omic profiles. The primary analysis will compare 'resilient' carriers (disease-free after 10+ years past median onset) to 'delayed-onset' controls (who develop disease during follow-up), using polygenic risk scores, epigenetic age acceleration (Horvath clock), and Mendelian Randomization to test for causal protective factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, prospective estimates of true genetic resilience, moving beyond cross-sectional 'unaffected carrier' studies. It will identify specific protective genetic variants, epigenetic signatures, and modifiable environmental factors with high confidence, directly informing the development of preventative therapeutic strategies. The 'delayed-onset' control group design sets a new methodological standard for resilience research.
proposals.novelty: The critical innovation is the prospective inclusion of a matched control group of carriers who later develop the disease ('delayed-onset'), a design that directly addresses the major confounding factor in prior resilience studies and isolates factors specific to lifelong protection rather than mere later onset.

proposals.title: Genetic Resilience to Fracture: A Genome-Wide Association Study of Protective Modifiers in High-Risk Carriers of Bone Mineral Density Variants
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified variants associated with increased risk of complex diseases like osteoporosis and fracture, but they primarily explain disease occurrence. A critical, understudied question is why many individuals carrying high-risk genetic variants remain disease-free. Identifying factors that confer resilience in these genetically predisposed individuals could reveal novel biological pathways for protection, offering targets for intervention that differ from those derived from studying disease etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unaffected carriers of established high-risk genetic variants for osteoporotic fracture possess other genetic or genomic factors (e.g., protective alleles, favorable polygenic scores, or gene-gene interactions) that mitigate their inherited risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a case-only genetic study within large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank). 'Cases' are defined as individuals carrying a high genetic risk burden (e.g., top decile of a fracture polygenic risk score or carriers of specific GWAS-identified risk variants like those near SOST or in the 16q24 locus) but who are free of radiographic vertebral and hip fractures. We will perform a genome-wide association study within this resilient group, testing for variants associated with higher bone mineral density (BMD) measured by DXA compared to other high-risk carriers with lower BMD. Secondary analyses will examine non-genetic factors (e.g., physical activity, nutrient intake) and test for gene-environment interactions using regression models.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond risk estimation to directly map genetic modifiers of resilience, a paradigm shift from disease-focused genetics. It will generate specific, testable hypotheses about biological mechanisms that buffer against genetic risk. The findings could inform the development of 'resilience-mimicking' therapies and refine risk prediction by identifying protective subgroups.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Ioannidis and others has focused on risk variant discovery and population differences in genetic effect sizes, this proposal explicitly targets the genetic architecture of protection within a high-risk genomic context, a design distinct from standard case-control GWAS.

proposals.title: Genetic Deconstruction of Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: A Pleiotropy-Informed Subphenotype Analysis
proposals.motivation: Clinical diagnoses like Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) and its complications, such as Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA), represent heterogeneous syndromes with likely diverse underlying biological pathways. This heterogeneity obscures genetic associations and hampers targeted therapy development. Pre-2026 work in neurodegenerative disease (e.g., LRRK2 in Parkinson's) demonstrates how pleiotropic risk variants can delineate specific mechanistic subtypes, a paradigm largely untested in complex autoimmune disease subphenotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: Pleiotropic genetic risk variants, exemplified by LRRK2 G2019S or other variants implicated in immune regulation and inflammation, will associate not with the broad diagnosis of SLE or AIHA, but with a specific, mechanistically coherent clinical or serological subphenotype within SLE-associated AIHA.
proposals.method: We will perform a genetic association study in a well-phenotyped cohort of SLE patients with and without AIHA. The primary analysis will test the association of the LRRK2 G2019S variant and a curated set of pleiotropic variants from prior cross-trait GWAS with detailed subphenotypes (e.g., Coombs test subtype, complement levels, specific extra-hematologic manifestations). We will employ logistic regression adjusted for principal components, followed by Mendelian Randomization (using pre-2026 methods) to assess potential causal relationships between genetic liability and subphenotype expression, contrasting results against the broad diagnostic categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept that genetic architecture, specifically pleiotropic risk variants, can be used to deconstruct clinically homogeneous autoimmune entities into biologically distinct subgroups. It will generate specific hypotheses about mechanistic pathways in AIHA subphenotypes and demonstrate a novel analytical framework for bridging genetic findings across disease domains (neurodegenerative and autoimmune) to refine disease classification.
proposals.novelty: The approach transposes a genetic 'scaling' strategy from neurodegenerative disease research to a classic autoimmune complication, using genetics not merely for risk prediction but as a tool for active disease deconstruction. It moves beyond associating variants with broad diagnoses to testing their specific alignment with precise clinical-biological constructs.

proposals.title: Cross-Trait Genetic Association Analysis of BMI-Associated Variants and Substance Use Disorders: A Mendelian Randomization and Polygenic Risk Score Study
proposals.motivation: The pre-2026 finding of shared genetic architecture between obesity and nicotine addiction suggests a broader biological basis for addictive behaviors. Substance use disorders (SUDs) for alcohol and drugs represent a major public health burden with complex, partially genetic etiology. Testing whether established BMI genetic variants also associate with other SUDs could reveal novel, shared biological pathways and inform whether interventions for obesity might have pleiotropic effects on addiction risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of common genetic variants associated with body mass index (BMI) will show pleiotropic associations with alcohol use disorder and drug addiction, and that genetic predisposition to higher BMI will be causally associated with increased risk for these SUDs.
proposals.method: We will perform a cross-trait genome-wide association study (GWAS) meta-analysis using summary statistics from large-scale consortia (e.g., GIANT for BMI, PGC for SUDs). We will employ Mendelian Randomization (MR) using BMI-associated SNPs as instrumental variables to test for causal effects on SUDs, applying inverse-variance weighted, MR-Egger, and weighted median methods to assess robustness. Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for BMI will be calculated in independent cohorts with SUD phenotypes to test for association, controlling for relevant covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically quantify the shared genetic liability between BMI and major non-nicotine SUDs, moving beyond observational correlations. It will provide evidence for or against a causal role of obesity-related biology in addiction risk, which could shift etiological models and highlight potential targets for preventive or therapeutic strategies applicable across conditions.
proposals.novelty: While genetic overlap between BMI and smoking is known, a formal MR and PRS investigation extending this to alcohol and drug addictions is novel. This directly tests the generalizability of the 'common biological basis' hypothesis beyond nicotine, addressing a critical gap.

proposals.title: Modulation of Shared Genetic Risk for Obesity and Smoking by Socioeconomic and Built Environment: A Gene-Environment Interaction Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified shared genetic variants influencing both obesity and smoking behaviors, suggesting a common biological basis. However, the population-level incidence of these traits varies dramatically, indicating a strong role for modifiable environmental factors. Quantifying how specific environmental exposures interact with this shared genetic architecture is critical for understanding the etiology of these major public health risks and for moving beyond genetic association to actionable intervention targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that socioeconomic status (SES) and features of the built environment (e.g., walkability, fast-food outlet density) significantly modify the effect of polygenic risk scores (PRS) for the shared biology of obesity and smoking, with stronger genetic effects observed in more adverse environments.
proposals.method: We will construct a PRS for the shared liability using summary statistics from the largest pre-2026 GWAS meta-analyses for BMI and smoking initiation. This PRS will be calculated in large, deeply phenotyped cohorts with linked environmental data (e.g., UK Biobank). We will employ regression models with interaction terms between the PRS and geocoded environmental indices (SES, neighborhood deprivation, food environment). To distinguish true interaction from scale effects, we will use both linear and generalized linear models with appropriate link functions, and apply false discovery rate control for multiple testing across environmental exposures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, quantitative estimates of how specific, modifiable environmental factors amplify or dampen the common genetic risk for obesity and smoking. It will advance the field from documenting genetic overlap to modeling explicit GxE mechanisms. The findings will inform targeted public health strategies by identifying environmental contexts where genetic risk is most potent.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established genetic correlation, this study explicitly tests for interaction with concrete, policy-relevant environmental variables at scale. It applies Ioannidis's focus on rigorous estimation of genetic 'effect' on disease incidence between groups to the novel context of GxE within a shared biology framework.

proposals.title: Whole-Genome Sequencing to Identify Rare Variants Underlying Shared Biological Risk for Osteoporotic Fracture and Cardiometabolic Disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified common variants with small effects on complex traits, but they have limited power to detect rare, high-impact variants. Furthermore, epidemiological studies suggest shared biological pathways, like those involving Wnt signaling or mesenchymal stem cell differentiation, may simultaneously influence fracture risk and cardiometabolic conditions. A comprehensive analysis of rare variation across these shared pathways is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Rare, protein-altering variants in genes within biological pathways previously implicated in both bone mineral density and cardiometabolic traits (e.g., obesity, nicotine addiction) contribute significantly to the risk of osteoporotic fractures, and their aggregate effects explain a portion of the missing heritability and phenotypic correlation.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome sequencing (WGS) on approximately 10,000 individuals from existing cohorts with deep phenotypic data on vertebral fractures, bone density, and cardiometabolic traits. Analysis will focus on identifying rare (MAF < 0.01) coding and non-coding variants. We will employ gene-based burden and sequence kernel association tests (SKAT/SKAT-O) to aggregate rare variants within predefined biological pathways from prior work. Polygenic risk scores from common variants will be used for stratification to isolate the rare variant signal.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first WGS-based map of rare variants contributing to fracture risk via shared biological mechanisms. It will quantify the contribution of rare variants to disease co-occurrence and missing heritability, moving beyond common variant GWAS. The findings could identify novel, high-effect drug targets within these shared pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work used GWAS to find common variant overlaps, this proposal directly tests the role of rare variants in shared biology using WGS, a more powerful approach for low-frequency, high-penetrance effects. It integrates pathway-based aggregation with phenotypic correlation analysis, a method not yet widely applied to fracture genetics.

proposals.title: Disentangling Genetic and Therapeutic Effects on Survival in HIV: A Mendelian Randomization Study in the cART Era
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genetic association studies have identified variants, such as CCR5-Δ32, linked to slower HIV progression and survival benefit in the pre-therapy era. With the widespread use of effective combination antiretroviral therapy (cART), the clinical relevance and independent effect size of these genetic factors are unclear. Determining whether genetic effects persist is crucial for understanding residual disease heterogeneity, informing prognostic models, and identifying novel therapeutic targets beyond viral suppression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the survival benefit associated with specific host genetic variants (e.g., in CCR5, HLA, CXCR6) is independent of and additive to the effect of cART, and that its magnitude has diminished but remains statistically and clinically significant in treated populations.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage Mendelian Randomization (MR) design using individual-level data from large HIV cohorts (e.g., COHERE, NA-ACCORD). First, we will conduct a genome-wide survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) for all-cause mortality in cART-treated individuals, adjusting for therapy adherence and year of initiation. Second, we will use significant variants as instrumental variables in MR analyses to estimate the causal effect of the genetic trait on survival, explicitly testing for interaction between genetic risk scores and cART era/regimen. Sensitivity analyses will include Egger regression and weighted median estimators to assess pleiotropy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, quantitative estimate of the independent genetic contribution to survival in the modern treatment era. It will clarify whether known genetic associations represent historical artifacts or enduring biological mechanisms, directly informing the design of genetically-stratified care and the prioritization of host-targeted adjuvant therapies.
proposals.novelty: While genetic associations with HIV progression are well-documented, applying a causal inference framework (MR) to isolate genetic effects from potent therapeutic confounding in the cART era is novel. This moves beyond simple association to address a specific, unresolved question of effect modification by therapy.

proposals.title: A Multi-Ancestry Genome-Wide Association and Population Genetics Study of the CCR5-Δ32 Variant: Quantifying Differential Frequencies and Fracture Risk Associations
proposals.motivation: The CCR5-Δ32 allele is a well-characterized loss-of-function variant with established protective effects against HIV infection and potential pleiotropic associations with other diseases, including fracture risk. However, its frequency and phenotypic effects have been studied predominantly in populations of European descent, creating a significant evidence gap. Quantifying its global distribution and health associations in diverse populations is critical for assessing the generalizability of genetic risk predictions and understanding population-specific disease etiologies, a core challenge in genetic epidemiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CCR5-Δ32 allele exhibits significant heterogeneity in both population frequency and in the magnitude/direction of its association with fracture risk across non-Caucasian populations, and that these differences are not fully explained by genetic ancestry principal components alone.
proposals.method: We will perform a coordinated analysis of existing whole-genome and exome sequencing data from large, diverse cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us, PAGE study, and non-European biobanks). We will directly genotype the CCR5-Δ32 deletion using sequence data. Allele frequency will be calculated per population group. Association with incident radiographic vertebral fractures (a challenging, pre-defined phenotype from Ioannidis's prior work) will be tested using logistic regression within each major ancestry group (e.g., African, East Asian, South Asian, Hispanic/Latino), adjusting for age, sex, and genetic principal components. We will then apply meta-analytic and cross-ancestry fine-mapping methods (e.g., MR-MEGA) to formally test for heterogeneity of effect sizes and to estimate the variant's contribution to differences in fracture incidence between populations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative map of CCR5-Δ32 frequency and fracture risk association across global populations, directly addressing a key diversity gap in human genetics. It will yield insights into the population genetics of this variant and test the generalizability of a specific genetic risk factor for an important clinical outcome, informing more equitable precision medicine strategies.
proposals.novelty: While the variant is well-known, a systematic, large-scale analysis of its fracture risk association specifically in non-Caucasian populations, using a rigorously defined fracture phenotype and state-of-the-art cross-population genetic methods, has not been conducted. This moves beyond descriptive frequency reports to formally test heterogeneity in a clinically relevant pleiotropic effect.

proposals.title: A Genome-Wide Interaction Study to Identify Genetic Modifiers of the CCR5-Δ32 HIV Survival Phenotype
proposals.motivation: The CCR5-Δ32 homozygous genotype confers strong resistance to HIV-1 infection, but its role in disease progression among infected individuals is less clear. A subset of individuals with HIV-1 infection and the CCR5-Δ32 allele exhibit exceptional long-term survival without treatment, suggesting other host genetic factors may interact with CCR5-Δ32 to modulate disease severity. Identifying these genetic modifiers is critical for understanding the full spectrum of host-pathogen interactions and could reveal novel pathways for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific host genetic variants, beyond the CCR5-Δ32 mutation, interact with it to enable prolonged survival and non-progression in HIV-1 infected individuals, and that these can be identified through genome-wide interaction analysis.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-stage genome-wide association study (GWAS) focused on genetic interactions. First, we will conduct a discovery analysis in a cohort of HIV+ long-term survivors (LTS) with at least one CCR5-Δ32 allele, using HIV+ rapid progressors without Δ32 as controls. We will employ a genome-wide interaction scan using a logistic regression framework testing for SNP*Δ32 interaction effects on the LTS phenotype, adjusting for principal components. Top hits (p < 5x10^-8) will be validated in an independent cohort. Secondary analyses will use Mendelian Randomization approaches to assess potential causal relationships between implicated pathways and HIV progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the established main effect of CCR5-Δ32 to map the polygenic interactive landscape enabling exceptional HIV survival. It will provide a novel set of genetic targets for functional validation and could refine our understanding of HIV pathogenesis. The methodological approach also contributes to the field of genetic interaction analysis for complex disease outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While CCR5-Δ32's role in infection resistance is well-known, systematically searching for its genetic interactors in the context of disease progression is novel. This proposal shifts the focus from a single protective variant to a network of modifiers, addressing the incomplete penetrance of the Δ32 survival phenotype.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Predictive Performance and Clinical Utility of Machine Learning Models Integrating CCR5, CCR2, and Other Genetic Variants for Fracture Risk
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous genetic loci associated with complex diseases and traits, including bone mineral density and fracture risk. However, the translation of polygenic risk scores (PRS) into clinically useful tools for predicting individual disease progression remains limited, with typical effect sizes being modest. This gap is critical to address for improving preventive strategies in conditions like osteoporosis, where accurate risk stratification can guide interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models that integrate specific candidate gene variants (e.g., CCR5, CCR2) implicated in bone metabolism and inflammation with broader polygenic risk information will demonstrate superior predictive accuracy for radiographic vertebral fracture progression compared to conventional PRS or single-variant models, but their clinical utility will be constrained by modest effect sizes and potential for miscalibration across populations.
proposals.method: We will train and validate models using existing large-scale cohort data (e.g., UK Biobank, Framingham) with genomic and longitudinal fracture phenotype data. We will employ and compare specific algorithms including regularized regression (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost). Models will be built on a base of common GWAS-derived variants for bone traits, with forced inclusion of the CCR5-Δ32, CCR2-V64I, and other pre-specified candidate variants. Performance will be rigorously assessed via calibration, discrimination (C-statistic), and net reclassification improvement, with explicit evaluation of heterogeneity across ancestry groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, pre-registered evaluation of whether integrating pathway-specific candidate genes into ML-based risk models offers a meaningful advantage over standard PRS for fracture prediction. It will also quantify the potential for model miscalibration and differential performance across populations, directly addressing Ioannidis's prior work on challenges in translating genetic associations to population risk differences.
proposals.novelty: While ML for genetic risk prediction is not new, a focused, skeptical evaluation centered on specific, biologically plausible candidate genes (CCR5/CCR2) within a polygenic framework, with an explicit hypothesis about their limited additive value and a primary analysis plan focused on calibration and transportability, is a novel and methodologically rigorous approach.

proposals.title: A Genome-Wide Association Study of Host Genetic Variants Influencing the Rate of HIV Disease Progression to AIDS
proposals.motivation: Despite effective antiretroviral therapy, understanding the natural history of HIV infection remains crucial for vaccine design and identifying novel therapeutic targets. Pre-2026 candidate gene studies have identified a few host genetic factors (e.g., CCR5-Δ32, HLA variants) associated with HIV acquisition or progression, but these explain only a fraction of the observed heterogeneity in disease course. A comprehensive, unbiased genome-wide scan is needed to discover novel variants and biological pathways influencing HIV pathogenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genome-wide association study (GWAS) will identify novel, common genetic variants beyond known candidate genes that are significantly associated with the rate of CD4+ T-cell decline and progression to AIDS in untreated HIV-1 infection.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-ancestry GWAS meta-analysis using individual-level genetic data (imputed to the TOPMed reference panel) and meticulously curated longitudinal phenotypic data from established HIV cohorts (e.g., MACS, ALIVE, IeDEA). The primary phenotype will be the slope of CD4+ decline pre-ART, analyzed using linear mixed models adjusting for population stratification, age, and sex. Secondary analyses will employ Cox proportional hazards models for time to AIDS-defining events. We will use standard GWAS tools (PLINK, SAIGE) and apply stringent significance thresholds (p < 5e-8). Post-GWAS, we will conduct fine-mapping, colocalization with eQTL data, and pathway enrichment analyses.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, well-powered GWAS map of host genetic determinants of HIV progression, moving beyond candidate genes. It will deliver a prioritized list of novel loci and genes for functional validation, potentially revealing new biological mechanisms of viral control or immune deterioration. The findings could inform the development of host-directed therapies and refine prognostic models.
proposals.novelty: While GWAS has been applied to HIV acquisition, a focused large-scale GWAS on the *rate of disease progression*—a critical and distinct phenotype—remains understudied. This proposal leverages Ioannidis's expertise in complex trait genetics, rigorous phenotype definition (as in fracture GWAS), and methods for cross-population analysis to address a high-impact question with a robust, hypothesis-free approach.

proposals.title: Inverse Genotype Inference: A Bayesian Framework for Estimating Likely CCR5/CCR2 Genotypes from HIV-1 Disease Progression Kinetics
proposals.motivation: The CCR5-Δ32 and CCR2-64I variants are well-established genetic modifiers of HIV-1 acquisition and disease progression, yet their population-specific frequencies and effects on progression kinetics are heterogeneous. Existing genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for disease phenotypes like fracture risk highlight the challenges of defining progression outcomes. An inverse model that infers likely genotypes from observed progression data could address scenarios where direct genotyping is unavailable or impractical, such as in historical cohorts or resource-limited settings, and refine estimates of genetic contribution to population differences in disease incidence.
proposals.hypothesis: The trajectory of key disease progression markers (e.g., CD4+ T-cell decline, time to AIDS) contains sufficient information to probabilistically infer an individual's likely CCR5/CCR2 genotype status, conditional on known epidemiological parameters and genetic architecture.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model. The forward model will parameterize HIV-1 progression kinetics using a non-linear mixed-effects framework, with progression rates informed by pre-2026 meta-analyses of genotype-specific disease courses. The inverse inference will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, specifically a Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) algorithm implemented in Stan, to compute the posterior probability distribution of diploid genotypes (e.g., Δ32/Δ32, Δ32/WT, WT/WT) given an individual's longitudinal biomarker data and prior genotype frequencies from the 1000 Genomes Project. Model validation will be performed on genotyped cohorts with longitudinal clinical data (e.g., MACS, ALIVE).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological tool for genetic epidemiology, enabling the estimation of genetic risk architecture from phenotypic time-series data. It will contribute to understanding the 'missing heritability' in infectious disease progression by formally linking dynamic clinical phenotypes to specific genetic variants. The framework can later be generalized to other genotype-phenotype kinetics problems beyond HIV.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of genetic effect on disease risk are standard, inverting the problem to infer genotype from detailed progression kinetics is a non-trivial, under-explored approach. This moves beyond static risk association to dynamic, model-based genetic inference, aligning with Ioannidis's focus on challenging phenotype definitions and the contribution of variants to population disease differences.

proposals.title: Dissecting the PARK3 Locus: A Fine-Mapping and Colocalization Study to Identify the Causal Gene Underlying the Linkage Signal for Parkinson's Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: The PARK3 locus on chromosome 2p13 has been linked to autosomal dominant Parkinson's disease (PD) for over two decades, yet the causative gene remains elusive. Identifying the true risk mediator is critical for understanding PD pathogenesis and developing targeted interventions. This unresolved linkage represents a classic challenge in genetic epidemiology where the initially implicated gene may not be the true causal variant, necessitating a systematic re-evaluation of the locus.
proposals.hypothesis: The PARK3 linkage signal is not primarily driven by variation in the previously suspected gene (e.g., SPR) but is mediated by a different gene or regulatory element within the locus. This mediating gene's effect can be detected through its association with PD risk and correlated endophenotypes.
proposals.method: We will perform high-resolution fine-mapping of the PARK3 region using whole-genome sequencing and imputed GWAS data from large PD consortia (e.g., IPDGC). We will apply statistical fine-mapping methods (e.g., SuSiE) to identify credible sets of causal variants. Subsequently, we will conduct colocalization analyses (using tools like COLOC) between PD risk and quantitative trait loci (QTLs) for gene expression (eQTLs), splicing (sQTLs), and protein abundance (pQTLs) from relevant brain tissues (e.g., substantia nigra) to pinpoint the most likely mediating gene. Mendelian Randomization will be used to test causal effects of candidate gene expression on PD risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will resolve a longstanding ambiguity in PD genetics, identifying the true risk gene at the PARK3 locus. It will provide a methodological template for re-investigating other unresolved linkage peaks in complex diseases. The findings could reveal a novel therapeutic target or biological pathway for Parkinson's disease.
proposals.novelty: While fine-mapping is standard for GWAS signals, its rigorous application to an old, complex linkage region like PARK3, integrated with multi-omics QTL colocalization, is a novel synthesis. The approach directly tests the non-obvious premise that the linkage signal has been misattributed, moving beyond simply searching for stronger associations within the known candidate.

proposals.title: Validation of the rs1876487 SPR Locus Association with Vertebral Fracture Risk in Scandinavian Cohorts: A Replication and Fine-Mapping Study
proposals.motivation: The SPR gene locus (rs1876487) has been implicated in bone mineral density (BMD) and fracture risk in genome-wide association studies (GWAS), but findings often lack robust replication in independent, well-phenotyped cohorts. This is critical for clinical translation, as non-replication is a major source of false-positive associations and wasted research resources. Scandinavian populations, with their extensive biobanks and detailed health registries, provide an ideal setting for validating this signal in a genetically homogeneous North European population with high osteoporosis prevalence.
proposals.hypothesis: The rs1876487 variant (or its linked proxies) in the SPR locus is significantly associated with radiographic vertebral fracture risk in Scandinavian populations, independently of and/or through its effect on BMD.
proposals.method: We will perform a targeted genetic association study using existing genotype data from large Scandinavian cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, FinnGen, Danish Blood Donor Study). The primary analysis will test the association between rs1876487 (imputed or directly genotyped) and radiographically confirmed vertebral fracture, using logistic regression adjusted for age, sex, and genetic principal components. Secondary analyses will assess the association's independence from BMD (using mediation analysis) and conduct fine-mapping via Bayesian methods (e.g., FINEMAP) within the 16q24 locus to identify potential causal variants. Sensitivity analyses will evaluate potential confounding by population stratification and other fracture risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive replication (or refutation) of the SPR locus association with vertebral fractures in a large, independent population, directly addressing the reproducibility crisis in genetic epidemiology. It will clarify whether this signal is a robust genetic risk factor for osteoporotic fracture and elucidate its relationship with BMD. The results will inform the prioritization of this locus for functional follow-up and potential therapeutic target development.
proposals.novelty: While the 16q24 locus is known for BMD, this study focuses specifically on the clinically relevant endpoint of radiographic vertebral fracture in a targeted replication, moving beyond BMD surrogates. The application of fine-mapping in Scandinavian cohorts, known for reduced haplotype diversity, offers a unique opportunity to refine the causal signal more precisely than in mixed-population GWAS.

proposals.title: Haplotype-Based Investigation of Founder Effects and Causal Variants in the PARK3 Locus for Parkinson's Disease
proposals.motivation: The PARK3 locus, mapped to chromosome 2p13, has been linked to autosomal dominant Parkinson's disease (PD) in families of European descent, but the precise pathogenic variant(s) remain elusive. Identifying the causal mutation is critical for understanding the biological mechanisms of this form of PD and for accurate genetic counseling. A founder effect analysis, leveraging pre-2026 genetic epidemiology methods, offers a powerful strategy to narrow the search within this large linkage region by identifying shared haplotypes indicative of a common ancestral mutation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the PARK3-linked families share a distinct, extended haplotype around the 2p13 locus, originating from a single founder mutation, and that targeted sequencing of this shared region will reveal a rare, high-penetrance coding or regulatory variant responsible for the disease in these pedigrees.
proposals.method: We will perform high-density SNP genotyping and haplotype reconstruction in the original families used to map PARK3, using algorithms for identity-by-descent (IBD) segment detection (e.g., GERMLINE, PLINK). The minimal shared haplotype will define the critical region. Within this region, we will conduct whole-exome and targeted deep sequencing (using Illumina platforms) in key affected individuals. Variants will be prioritized based on segregation, predicted functional impact (using pre-2026 tools like PolyPhen-2, SIFT, and CADD), and absence from population databases (gnomAD).
proposals.contribution: This study will either identify the long-sought PARK3 causal variant, providing a direct target for functional studies, or will rigorously exclude a single founder effect, reshaping the genetic model of the locus. It will demonstrate the utility of combining classic linkage families with modern sequencing for solving old genetic mysteries, directly contributing to the field of neurodegenerative disease genetics.
proposals.novelty: While genome-wide association studies (GWAS) dominate PD genetics, this proposal returns to the original families with a specific founder-effect hypothesis, applying contemporary sequencing to a classic linkage problem—a targeted approach distinct from agnostic screening of large cohorts.

proposals.title: Functional Characterization of SPR Promoter and 3' UTR Variants Associated with Fracture Risk: A Causal Inference Study
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified non-coding variants in the sepiapterin reductase (SPR) gene locus associated with vertebral fracture risk and bone mineral density, but their functional impact remains unknown. Determining the molecular consequences of these promoter and 3' UTR polymorphisms is critical for moving from statistical association to causal mechanism, a core challenge in post-GWAS research. This aligns with Ioannidis's focus on rigorous analysis of genetic risk factors for complex diseases like osteoporosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific SPR promoter and 3' UTR variants identified in fracture GWAS causally alter SPR gene expression levels by modulating transcriptional activity and/or mRNA stability, thereby influencing bone metabolism pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step functional genomics pipeline. First, we will clone the major haplotypes encompassing the target variants into luciferase reporter vectors for promoter and 3' UTR assays in osteoblast-like cell lines (e.g., SaOS-2). Second, we will use CRISPR/Cas9-mediated base editing to introduce specific alleles into these cell lines and quantify endogenous SPR mRNA and protein expression via qRT-PCR and western blot. Third, we will perform allele-specific expression analysis in human primary osteoblasts from genotyped donors to validate regulatory effects in a physiological context.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly establish a molecular mechanism linking specific SPR non-coding variants to gene regulation, providing a causal link between GWAS signals and a potential biological pathway for fracture risk. It will contribute a functional template for interpreting genetic associations with complex, challenging phenotypes like fractures, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic understanding.
proposals.novelty: While SPR's role in bone is not fully characterized, this study uniquely focuses on functionally testing non-coding variants from a fracture GWAS in a relevant cellular context, applying a causal inference framework favored by Ioannidis to a specific genetic locus of skeletal disease.

proposals.title: Admixture Mapping of Ancestry-Specific Genetic Risk for Parkinson's Disease in Admixed Populations
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for Parkinson's disease (PD) have been overwhelmingly conducted in populations of European ancestry, limiting the generalizability of findings and potentially missing risk variants with differing frequencies or effects across ancestral groups. This bias hinders the understanding of PD etiology and the development of equitable precision medicine. Admixed populations, such as African Americans and Latinos, offer a powerful opportunity to localize ancestry-specific risk loci by leveraging the correlation between local ancestry and disease risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that admixture mapping in admixed populations will identify specific genomic regions where local ancestry is significantly associated with PD risk, revealing novel ancestry-specific genetic variants that are not detectable in standard GWAS of homogeneous populations.
proposals.method: We will apply admixture mapping to existing PD case-control cohorts of admixed populations (e.g., African American, Latino). Local ancestry will be inferred using pre-2026 reference panels (e.g., 1000 Genomes) and established algorithms like RFMix or LAMP. We will perform a genome-wide scan for association between local ancestry at each locus and PD status, using logistic regression models adjusted for global ancestry and relevant covariates. Significant loci will be fine-mapped, and candidate variants will be prioritized by integrating functional genomic data and performing conditional analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly identify genomic regions harboring ancestry-specific PD risk factors, moving beyond simple replication of known loci. It will provide a more complete genetic architecture of PD and generate specific, testable hypotheses about population differences in disease incidence. The findings will inform the biological basis of PD disparities and highlight variants for functional follow-up.
proposals.novelty: While admixture mapping has been applied to other complex traits, its systematic application to PD in admixed populations remains limited. This approach directly tests for genetic risk factors that differ in frequency by ancestry, a question orthogonal to standard GWAS, and leverages population structure as a tool for discovery rather than a confounder.

proposals.title: Ancestry-Aware Machine Learning for Fracture Risk Prediction: Integrating SPR Variants into Polygenic Models
proposals.motivation: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for fracture risk, primarily derived from European ancestry data, show poor transferability across populations, exacerbating health disparities. The recent identification of SPR variants associated with radiographic vertebral fractures provides a crucial, biologically relevant signal, but their integration into ancestry-specific predictive models remains unexplored. This gap limits the clinical utility of genetic risk prediction for a major public health outcome like osteoporotic fracture.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating SPR variants with other genetic data via ancestry-specific machine learning models will generate polygenic risk scores with superior predictive accuracy and improved portability across diverse populations compared to standard, linear PRS methods.
proposals.method: We will develop ancestry-stratified models using data from the UK Biobank and other large biobanks. We will employ penalized regression (LASSO, elastic net) and non-linear tree-based methods (random forests, gradient boosting machines) to integrate SPR variants with genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics and individual-level genetic data. Model performance for predicting incident fracture will be rigorously validated across ancestries, with careful calibration to account for differing allele frequencies and linkage disequilibrium patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new generation of ancestry-tailored PRS for fracture, directly addressing the portability crisis in genetic epidemiology. It will provide a framework for integrating novel, biologically informed variants like SPR into machine learning models to improve risk stratification. The results will critically inform the feasibility and equity of using PRS in diverse clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While PRS are common, this proposal uniquely focuses on the integration of a specific, challenging fracture phenotype variant (SPR) using non-linear machine learning methods explicitly optimized for different ancestries, moving beyond simple linear weighting of GWAS hits.

ideas.title: Calibrating Variant Impact via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling to determine the clinical relevance of novel risk variants. In ecology, the impact of a new factor on a species is assessed by integrating it into a model of suitable habitat defined by many environmental variables. Analogously, we will construct a multi-parametric model of the 'clinical phenotype niche' using population genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic data from patient cohorts with known disease trajectories. The novel variants p.A419V and p.M1646T will be introduced as new model parameters. Their clinical relevance will be quantified by the degree to which they alter the model's accuracy in predicting key outcomes like age of onset or rate of progression, thereby calibrating their functional impact within a complex biological system.

A critical innovation is the inclusion of a matched control group of carriers who develop the disease later ('delayed-onset'), enabling direct comparison to isolate factors specific to lifelong resilience. The integrated analysis of genetic, epigenetic, environmental, and multi-omic data within this robust design aims to identify mechanisms of true disease protection with high confidence.

ideas.description: This approach directly addresses the core weakness of statistical phasing by first employing long-read sequencing platforms (e.g., PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore) to perform direct molecular haplotyping on a strategically selected subset of individuals from the population of interest. This empirically derived, high-confidence haplotype map serves as a foundational anchor. Advanced statistical phasing tools are then applied to the broader cohort, but their outputs are continuously validated and corrected against this molecular anchor. The final system produces a refined and robust protective haplotype structure with quantified confidence levels, significantly reducing error-driven false positives and negatives.

ideas.title: Applying Rare Signal Detection from Astrophysics to Variant Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that allele frequency directly indicates clinical relevance by drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where rare, significant signals (like Earth-like exoplanets) are identified not by their prevalence but by their distinct pattern against background noise. The analogous solution involves model-based detection algorithms designed for specific, predicted signal signatures in massive datasets. Adapted to genomics, the research would develop and apply similar pattern-recognition algorithms to scan for rare variants with strong effects. These algorithms would model the multi-dimensional 'signature' of a clinically relevant variant—integrating factors like familial segregation, functional genomic annotations, extreme phenotypic effect in carriers, and evolutionary constraint—to systematically identify high-impact variants that are missed by standard frequency-based filtering in association studies.

ideas.title: Deconstructing Clinical Homogeneity in Autoimmune and Neurodegenerative Diseases via Cross-Domain Genetic Architecture
ideas.description: This research applies the genetic scaling approach from neurodegenerative disease (testing a specific variant like LRRK2 across disorders) to challenge the clinical homogeneity of a defined autoimmune entity, specifically AIHA in SLE. The core hypothesis is that LRRK2 or similar pleiotropic risk variants will associate not with the broad diagnosis of SLE or AIHA, but with a specific, mechanistically coherent clinical or serological phenotype within that population. This transforms a genetic association study into a tool for disease deconstruction, using genetic architecture to identify biologically distinct subgroups that may require different therapeutic strategies, thereby bridging neurodegenerative and autoimmune research paradigms.

ideas.title: Use Pedigree-Based Methods from Conservation Genetics to Control for Stratification in LRRK2 Studies
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using FBATs to control for population stratification in LRRK2 studies. By analogy, conservation genetics faces a parallel problem: estimating genetic parameters in wild, non-randomly mating populations with inherent substructure. That field solves it using pedigree-based quantitative genetic models (e.g., the animal model). We adapt this solution by applying a similar pedigree-based mixed model framework to LRRK2 research. This involves genotyping LRRK2 variants and genome-wide markers in individuals with known familial relationships (even extended pedigrees), constructing a kinship matrix, and then testing for association within the model that partitions phenotypic variance into genetic (LRRK2-specific and polygenic background) and residual components. This method inherently controls for population stratification through the pedigree structure, provides robust heritability estimates for the LRRK2 effect, and is more powerful than FBATs for quantitative endophenotypes.

ideas.title: Testing the Portability of a Protective Haplotype Using Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a protective haplotype's effect size is uniform by testing it with a method adapted from ecology. In ecology, a species' niche model, built in its native range, is projected to new environments to test niche conservatism. Here, we analogously model the haplotype's effect as a function of its local genetic background (e.g., interacting allele frequencies, background polygenic risk). We then project this model onto the distinct genetic 'landscapes' of other ancestries. If the effect size varies with genetic background, the projections will systematically deviate from the observed effects in validation cohorts, indicating the protective 'niche' is not conserved and the effect is not uniform.

These endotypes are then rigorously validated for association with distinct disease trajectories, treatment responses, and underlying immune-pathogenic mechanisms, aiming to deliver a taxonomy with direct translational relevance for personalized management.

ideas.title: Apply Composite Environmental Risk Score (CERS) methodology from ecology to LRRK2 variant aggregation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological risk assessment, where Composite Environmental Risk Scores (CERS) integrate multiple, heterogeneous stressors to predict ecosystem outcomes. The analogous problem is the need to combine diverse risk factors into a single predictive metric. By applying this methodology, we propose to develop a Composite LRRK2 Risk Score (CLRS). This score would not simply sum variant effects like a standard PRS but would use multivariate models from ecology to aggregate diverse LRRK2-related genetic factors—including coding variants, regulatory changes, and their potential interactions—into a unified metric. This adapted approach aims to provide a more nuanced and potentially more powerful tool for predicting Parkinson's disease risk and phenotypic heterogeneity associated with the LRRK2 locus than traditional polygenic risk score methods alone.

ideas.title: Inverse Seismology for Variants: Inferring Pathogenic Mechanism from Population and Phenotype Signatures
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to inverse problems in seismology to the original genomic challenge of predicting variant pathogenicity from population allele frequency and disease profiles. In seismology, the characteristics of an earthquake source are inferred from seismic wave patterns recorded at the surface. The analogous solution involves creating forward models of wave propagation and inverting them. Adapted to genomics, the 'source' is the pathogenic variant, and the 'observed waves' are its allele frequency spectrum across diverse populations and its associated disease phenotype signatures in biomedical databases. The research would develop a forward model that simulates how variants with different functional impacts (e.g., selective pressure, penetrance, mode of inheritance) generate these observable signatures. An inverse modeling or Bayesian inference framework would then be applied to these real-world signatures to predict the variant's underlying pathogenic function and clinical impact, effectively working backward from population-scale effects to variant-level causality.

ideas.title: Single-cell Mendelian Randomization for causal LRRK2 effects on autoimmune hemolytic anemia risk
ideas.description: This research direction applies the causal inference logic of Mendelian randomization (MR) to a single-cell resolution experimental system. It uses genetic variants in the LRRK2 locus as natural instruments to causally link LRRK2 function to the risk of autoimmune diseases like AIHA, but directly tests this by measuring variant effects on gene expression and signaling pathways within single, relevant immune cells (e.g., B cells, monocytes). This allows for the direct observation of the causal molecular chain from genetic instrument to cellular phenotype, moving beyond population-level associations to pinpoint the specific cell types and altered transcriptional programs through which a genetic risk factor like LRRK2 might influence autoantibody production or erythrocyte clearance, thereby mechanistically bridging genetics and autoimmune pathology.

ideas.title: Mechanism-based patient stratification for Parkinson's disease trials using integrated polygenic and multi-omic profiling
ideas.description: This approach defines patient subgroups for clinical trials by integrating polygenic risk scores with deep molecular and clinical phenotyping, rather than relying on a single rare mutation. It uses unsupervised machine learning on multi-modal data—including genetics, cerebrospinal fluid proteomics, neuroimaging, and digital motor assessments—to identify clusters of patients with shared biological pathways. This creates more inclusive and mechanistically defined cohorts for trials, increasing the likelihood of detecting a therapeutic signal by targeting specific disease mechanisms present across a broader patient population. The system is designed to be adaptive, refining subgroup definitions as new data from ongoing trials and biomarker studies become available.

ideas.description: This study applies Mendelian randomization (MR) principles from genetic epidemiology to the clinical question of treatment response in SLE-associated AIHA. We will use genetic variants associated with antiphospholipid antibody (aPL) positivity as instrumental variables to infer the causal effect of aPL status on measures of treatment resistance (e.g., time to remission, need for second-line therapy). This approach genetically 'pseudo-randomizes' patients by aPL liability, allowing a less confounded comparison than observational studies. The result will provide stronger evidence on whether aPL directly contributes to treatment-refractory disease, informing stratified management and mechanistic research.

ideas.title: Resilience Engineering in Genetic Systems: Identifying Natural Buffers in High-Risk Carriers
ideas.description: This idea applies the principles of resilience engineering from structural and materials science to the study of disease protection. In engineering, the key to understanding resilience lies in analyzing systems that withstand predicted failure points by identifying built-in redundancies, material properties, and design adaptations. Adapted to genetics, the research would investigate individuals who carry high-risk genetic variants but remain disease-free, conceptualizing them as naturally 'damage-tolerant' biological systems. The solution involves a systematic, multi-omics 'resilience audit' to map the compensatory mechanisms—such as modifier genes, robust network architectures, epigenetic buffering, and protective lifestyle factors—that absorb the pathogenic stress of the risk variant, thereby revealing endogenous pathways to disease resistance that could be therapeutically engineered in others.

ideas.title: Modeling LRRK2's Epistatic Network Using Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that LRRK2 variants act independently by explicitly modeling their epistatic interactions with other Parkinson's risk loci. It draws an analogy from community ecology, where a species' realized niche is defined by interactions with other species, not just its own traits. The analogous solution uses multivariate modeling on community data to map these interactions. Adapted to genetics, the research would treat the genome as an ecological landscape, employing network analysis and multivariate models (e.g., Bayesian epistatic regression) on large genomic datasets to map the 'epistatic niche' of LRRK2—defining the specific genetic backgrounds that exacerbate or mitigate its pathogenic effect, moving beyond single-gene, additive risk models.

ideas.title: Adapting Sky Survey Pipelines for Population-Scale Genomic Discovery
ideas.description: The original idea proposes scaling from targeted LRRK2 genotyping to whole-genome sequencing (WGS) in massive biobanks. An analogous problem was solved in astronomy when the field scaled from targeted telescope observations to massive, all-sky surveys. The analogous solution involved creating automated, high-throughput data processing pipelines to standardize data ingestion, calibration, and cataloging of billions of objects, enabling both planned research and serendipitous discovery. Adapting this, the research idea is to develop a standardized, automated bioinformatics 'survey pipeline' for population-scale WGS data. This pipeline would uniformly process raw sequencing data from biobanks, performing variant calling, rigorous quality control, and functional annotation to create a comprehensive, searchable genomic catalog. This infrastructure directly supports the scaled-up LRRK2 investigation while simultaneously enabling systematic, genome-wide discovery of novel genetic associations, structural variants, and polygenic risk architectures across the entire cohort.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning to Define AIHA Phenotypes in SLE
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from oncology, where genomic data and unsupervised machine learning were used to deconstruct broad cancer categories into molecularly distinct subtypes. The analogous problem of assuming homogeneity within a disease entity is directly parallel to the assumption that AIHA in SLE is a single clinical entity. By applying this solution, we propose assembling a multi-center cohort of SLE patients with AIHA and subjecting a high-dimensional dataset of clinical and serological variables to unsupervised clustering algorithms (e.g., k-means, hierarchical clustering, or Gaussian mixture models). This data-driven approach will define distinct phenotypic clusters, which can then be validated for differences in outcomes, treatment responses, and pathogenic mechanisms, fundamentally challenging the assumption of homogeneity.

ideas.title: Modeling epistatic interactions between LRRK2 and complement pathway genes in antiphospholipid-mediated red cell destruction
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether specific Parkinson's disease-associated genetic variants, particularly in LRRK2 and other risk loci, modify the biological mechanism and severity of hemolytic anemia driven by antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL). We hypothesize that epistatic interactions between these neurodegenerative risk genes and the complement or phagocytic pathways alter macrophage or microglial responses to aPL-opsonized red blood cells. By applying genetic interaction modeling from complex neurodegenerative disease to a defined autoimmune hematologic mechanism, the study seeks to identify genetic modifiers that confer resilience or susceptibility to antibody-mediated cell destruction, revealing shared pathophysiological nodes across disparate diseases.

ideas.title: A Causality-First, Multi-Cohort Framework for Robust LRRK2 Multi-Omics Integration and Prediction
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond generic ML integration by embedding causal reasoning and rigorous generalizability checks into the core pipeline. It begins by transforming high-dimensional omics data into pathway-centric features to enhance biological interpretability and reduce noise. The model is then developed and validated across multiple independent cohorts using adversarial domain adaptation to ensure learned patterns are robust to technical artifacts. Finally, causal inference methods are applied to the resulting integrative features to distinguish drivers of disease from correlative bystanders, yielding a predictive model with stronger biological plausibility and clinical translation potential.

ideas.title: Single-Cell LRRK2 Expression Profiling via Barcoded Viral Lineage Tracing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the lineage-tracing barcoding solution from developmental biology to solve the challenge of linking specific LRRK2 variants to their effects on gene expression in single neurons within a mixed population. The analogous solution uses viral vectors to deliver heritable, combinatorial fluorescent barcodes to trace cell lineage. Adapted here, adeno-associated viral (AAV) vectors would be engineered to deliver unique, inert RNA barcode sequences specific to each LRRK2 variant (e.g., G2019S, R1441C, wild-type). These barcodes are transcribed in infected neurons. During single-cell RNA-sequencing of brain tissue (e.g., from a transgenic or humanized mouse model or post-mortem human samples), both the cell's native transcriptome and the variant-specific barcode are captured. This allows for the precise pairing of a neuron's expressed LRRK2 variant with its full single-cell expression profile, enabling high-resolution analysis of variant-driven expression changes across different neuronal subtypes without the need for prior cell sorting or bulk analysis that obscures single-cell effects.

ideas.title: Single-cell multi-omic profiling of autoreactive B cells to define pathogenic trajectories from aPL to AIHA in SLE
ideas.description: This study proposes a longitudinal, single-cell multi-omic analysis of autoreactive B cells in SLE patients positive for antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL). By integrating single-cell RNA sequencing and B cell receptor sequencing, we will track clonal lineages over time, specifically measuring LRRK2 expression and variant status within single cells. This approach will test the hypothesis that aPL-producing B cell clones with high LRRK2 expression and specific genetic variants are predisposed to undergo further somatic hypermutation and class-switching, leading to the production of pathogenic warm autoantibodies that cause AIHA. The synergy lies in using a single-cell, gene-expression-focused methodology (from Idea B) to mechanistically deconstruct the temporal clinical relationship between aPL and AIHA (from Idea A) at the level of the pathogenic B cell clone.

ideas.title: Prospective cohort study on aPL and AIHA in SLE using a 'precipitation event' model from climatology
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from climatology, where the temporal relationship between a precursor condition and a subsequent event is established through long-term, threshold-based observational tracking. The analogous problem is linking a persistent atmospheric state to a drought; the solution is a prospective cohort study with precise definitions for the precursor. Adapted to SLE research, the study will be a multi-center prospective cohort following SLE patients without autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) at enrollment. We will define specific, quantitative thresholds for antiphospholipid antibody (aPL) positivity and clinically significant titer changes (the 'precursor'). Patients will be stratified based on this aPL status and followed with standardized, frequent assessments. The core adaptation is the explicit focus on modeling aPL as a precipitating event, analyzing the precise temporal sequence and latency period to establish if meeting the aPL threshold criteria predicts the subsequent 'event' of AIHA onset, thereby providing stronger evidence for a causal temporal relationship.

ideas.title: LRRK2 variant stratification of treatment response in aPL-positive SLE-associated AIHA
ideas.description: This research investigates whether specific LRRK2 genetic variants, known to modulate inflammatory vesicle trafficking and NLRP3 inflammasome activity, influence treatment resistance in SLE patients with autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) who are also antiphospholipid antibody (aPL)-positive. It hypothesizes that the pro-thrombotic and pro-inflammatory milieu of aPL positivity synergizes with hyperactive LRRK2 signaling pathways to create a distinct, treatment-refractory endotype. The study would stratify SLE-AIHA patients by both aPL status and LRRK2 variant profiles to compare responses to standard therapies (e.g., corticosteroids, rituximab). The ultimate goal is to define a high-risk patient subgroup that may benefit from the adjunctive use of emerging LRRK2 kinase inhibitors to overcome treatment resistance.

ideas.title: Dynamic aPL Profiling in AIHA: A Microbial Ecology-Inspired Approach to Challenge Static Exposure Classification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that aPL positivity is a static exposure in AIHA by drawing an analogy from microbial ecology. In that field, static presence/absence data of microbes has been superseded by dynamic, quantitative tracking of community fluctuations to understand disease pathogenesis. The analogous solution involves longitudinal, high-resolution metagenomic and qPCR profiling to correlate microbial dynamics with host phenotypes. Adapted to this context, the research would implement frequent, serial measurements of aPL titers and isotypes over the AIHA disease course, using highly sensitive and quantitative assays. This dynamic 'immunological profiling' data would be analyzed with ecological and time-series models to rigorously test for correlations between specific aPL fluctuation patterns (e.g., rapid titer rises, seroconversion) and changes in AIHA activity. The goal is to determine if aPL dynamics offer superior prognostic or diagnostic value compared to the current static classification, potentially leading to a paradigm shift in how laboratory risk is assessed in autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

ideas.title: Test if LRRK2-mediated microvascular endothelial dysfunction is a shared mechanism linking autoimmunity and neurodegeneration
ideas.description: This study proposes that gain-of-function LRRK2 variants, known to cause microvascular endothelial dysfunction and inflammasome activation, represent a shared subclinical mechanism linking two seemingly disparate disease domains. We hypothesize that this LRRK2-mediated endothelial pathology could be the 'thrombotic microangiopathy-like' substrate that directly damages red cells in antiphospholipid antibody (aPL) positive patients, while also contributing to blood-brain barrier disruption and neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease and ALS. The research would test this by analyzing LRRK2 variant status and biomarkers of endothelial activation in aPL-positive patients with cytopenias and in neurodegenerative disease cohorts, assessing whether LRRK2 activity modifies disease risk or phenotype across both groups. This approach seeks a parsimonious, genetically-informative mechanism that could explain vascular comorbidity in autoimmunity and neurodegeneration.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Deconvolution from Astrophysics to Refine Haplotype Phasing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a signal deconvolution solution from astrophysics to the problem of haplotype phasing in population genetics. In radio astronomy, the CLEAN algorithm is used to deconvolve blended signals from multiple sources to reconstruct a clear image. Analogously, we can treat observed genotype data as a noisy, blended signal of ancestral haplotypes. By adapting CLEAN's iterative framework—identifying the strongest haplotype signal, subtracting its predicted contribution given a recombination 'point-spread function', and iterating—we can develop a phasing tool that more accurately resolves protective haplotype structures, particularly in challenging datasets with low coverage or high admixture.
